a-little-princess.name: A Little Princess
a-little-princess.description:
Frances Hodgson Burnett's A Little Princess (1905) — the beloved story of Sara Crewe, a girl whose imagination and inner dignity sustain her through the cruelest reversal of fortune. From pampered pupil to attic servant and back to heiress, Sara proves that being a princess is about character, not circumstance. Structured as 19 chapters with thematic groupings exploring imagination, kindness, resilience, and the magic of compassion.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #146 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/146).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- Ethel Franklin Betts (1905, Charles Scribner's Sons) — original edition illustrator, elegant pen-and-ink drawings of Sara in both her wealthy and impoverished states
- Reginald Birch (1888, illustrated Little Lord Fauntleroy) — Burnett's preferred illustrator, whose style defined the visual world of her children's novels
- Harold Piffard (1905, Frederick Warne) — UK edition illustrations

adventures-of-pinocchio.name: The Adventures of Pinocchio
adventures-of-pinocchio.description:
Carlo Collodi's The Adventures of Pinocchio (Le Avventure di Pinocchio, 1883) -- the original Italian tale of a wooden puppet's long, painful, hilarious journey to become a real boy. Far darker and richer than the Disney adaptation, Collodi's Pinocchio is a picaresque epic: the puppet is hanged, enslaved, turned into a donkey, swallowed by a dogfish, and finally redeemed through labor and love. Thirty-six chapters in the Carol Della Chiesa translation, structured as story arcs and thematic groupings.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #500 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/500). English translation by Carol Della Chiesa.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- Enrico Mazzanti (1883, Felice Paggi) -- original illustrator, bold pen-and-ink drawings capturing the story's blend of comedy and menace
- Attilio Mussino (1911, Bemporad) -- definitive color illustrations, vivid watercolors that set the visual standard for Pinocchio worldwide
- Carlo Chiostri (1901, Bemporad) -- elegant, detailed ink illustrations
- Alice Carsey (1904, Ginn and Company) -- early American edition illustrations

aesops-fables.name: Aesop's Fables
aesops-fables.description:
The timeless moral fables attributed to Aesop, a legendary storyteller of ancient Greece (c. 620-564 BCE). Through talking animals, cunning foxes, proud lions, and foolish asses, these 284 short tales distill universal truths about human nature into stories children have loved for 2,500 years. V.S. Vernon Jones translation with introduction by G.K. Chesterton. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #21 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/21). 

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's 1912 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' (Heinemann) — widely available in public domain, featuring pen-and-ink drawings of foxes, lions, wolves, and other characters. Milo Winter's 1919 illustrations for 'The Aesop for Children' (Rand McNally) — full-color illustrations, public domain. Walter Crane's 1887 'The Baby's Own Aesop' — woodcut-style illustrations with rhyming morals, public domain. Ernest Griset's 1869 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' (Cassell) — detailed pen-and-ink, public domain. Francis Barlow's 1666 'Aesop's Fables with His Life' — the earliest major English illustrated edition, public domain. Richard Heighway's 1894 illustrations — pen-and-ink, public domain. Harrison Weir's 1867 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' — naturalistic animal drawings, public domain.

aesops-fables-storyboard.name: Aesop's Fables for Little Ones — Storyboard
aesops-fables-storyboard.description:
Production storyboard for animated series based on 30 of Aesop's most beloved fables. Each fable is a 5-minute episode split into 3 scenes with rhyming narration, AI image prompts, and camera directions. Designed for AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #21 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/21).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's 1912 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' (Heinemann) — pen-and-ink drawings with watercolor. Milo Winter's 1919 illustrations for 'The Aesop for Children' (Rand McNally) — full-color. Walter Crane's 1887 'The Baby's Own Aesop' — woodcut-style. Ernest Griset's 1869 illustrations (Cassell) — detailed pen-and-ink.

alchemy-ancient-modern.name: Alchemy: Ancient and Modern
alchemy-ancient-modern.description:
Alchemy: Ancient and Modern by H. Stanley Redgrove (1922, 2nd edition) — a comprehensive study of alchemistic doctrines, their relations to mysticism and modern physical science, with biographical accounts of noted alchemists.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #43240 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/43240)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The original book includes 16 full-page illustrations including a portrait of Paracelsus. Additional relevant imagery: alchemical emblems from Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens (1618), illustrations from the Splendor Solis (1582), and woodcuts from Basil Valentine's works.

alice-5-minute-stories.name: Alice in Wonderland — Illustrated Chapter Books
alice-5-minute-stories.description: Lewis Carroll's complete original text in ALL CAPS with 121 public domain illustrations. 12 printable landscape booklets (one per chapter). Each page has one illustration and a passage of text. Print landscape, fold in half for a picture book! Illustrated by Tenniel, Rackham, Carroll (manuscript), Hudson, Gutmann, Le Fanu, Walker, Rountree and more.

alice-in-the-amazon.name: Alice na Amazônia / Alice in the Amazon: The Forest Speaks in Verse
alice-in-the-amazon.description:
A decolonial retelling of Alice in Wonderland as poetry. Alice is a climate researcher who travels to the Amazon in 2025. She falls asleep by the Rio Negro and enters the forest's dream — where Curupira guards the roots, Iara sings the river's memory, Boto shapeshifts between worlds, and the Queen of Extraction plays croquet with human bodies. Each poem follows Carroll's chapter structure but replaces English nonsense with Amazonian cosmology: the forest is not wonderland — it is the original mind, older than language, and it has something to say to the girl who came to measure it. Written entirely in verse.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Amazonian botanical illustrations from Alfred Russel Wallace's 'A Narrative of Travels on the Amazon and Rio Negro' (1853). Illustrations from Henry Walter Bates' 'The Naturalist on the River Amazons' (1863). Ernst Haeckel's 'Kunstformen der Natur' (1904) — particularly jungle flora plates. Brazilian folk art woodcuts (xilogravura) from cordel literature tradition.

alice-in-the-mythlands.name: Alice in the Mythlands: Where the Stories Begin
alice-in-the-mythlands.description:
A series of rhyming poems in which Alice falls through a book of myths and lands — each time — in a different mythic world. She meets Raven stealing light from boxes, Anansi outwitting a god for stories, Sedna sinking to the sea floor with the whales growing from her fingers. She watches Amaterasu lured from a cave by laughter, plays ball with the Hero Twins in the Mayan underworld, and tries to keep up with Coyote who keeps dying and coming back. She descends through Inanna's seven gates and watches Elk call the wind to make dry land from water. The poems rhyme. They are silly and serious at the same time — like myths always are. Every poem links to its source myth in this repository, because the stories are real and older than the rhymes.

Source grammars: Myths Through Many Eyes, Myths of the North American Indians, Myths and Legends of the Great Plains, Myths of China and Japan, Myths of Babylonia and Assyria. All source texts are public domain.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's illustrations for various fairy tale collections (1900-1920). Warwick Goble's 'Indian Myth and Legend' illustrations (1913). Edmund Dulac's 'Stories from the Arabian Nights' and other collections (1907-1916). Kay Nielsen's 'East of the Sun and West of the Moon' illustrations (1914). Haida and Tlingit totem art from Smithsonian ethnographic collections. Mayan codex illustrations (public domain reproductions).

alice-in-the-mythlands-storyboard.name: Alice in the Mythlands: Where the Stories Begin — Storyboard
alice-in-the-mythlands-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on Alice in the Mythlands: Where the Stories Begin. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

alice-in-wonderland-chapter-book.name: Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland — A Chapter Book
alice-in-wonderland-chapter-book.description:
Lewis Carroll's beloved classic broken into scenes for multi-level reading. Level 2 chapters are emergent summaries (perfect for the littlest readers). Level 1 scenes contain the COMPLETE ORIGINAL TEXT alongside age-adapted retellings. Illustrated with public domain artwork from 5 artists: Lewis Carroll's own manuscript drawings (1864), Arthur Rackham (1907), Bessie Pease Gutmann (1933), Sir John Tenniel (1865), and Charles F.A. Voysey textile design (c.1930).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- Lewis Carroll original manuscript drawings (1864, British Library) - 42 pen-and-ink illustrations drawn by Carroll himself for Alice Liddell
- Arthur Rackham (1907, William Heinemann) - 13 color plates, art nouveau style
- Bessie Pease Gutmann (1933, Dodge Publishing) - 6 soft pastel illustrations
- Sir John Tenniel (1865, Macmillan) - the iconic original illustrations
- Charles F.A. Voysey (c.1930) - Arts & Crafts textile design featuring Alice characters

alice-s-tarot-my-5-years-old.name: Alice's Tarot (My 5 Years Old)
alice-s-tarot-my-5-years-old.description: This is a joint endeavour between myself and my daughter to create a tarot deck. It will mix Portuguese and English language, as we do at home :)

ancient-art-ritual.name: Ancient Art and Ritual
ancient-art-ritual.description:
Jane Ellen Harrison's groundbreaking study of the connection between ritual and art, tracing how Greek drama emerged from primitive seasonal rites. A founding text of the Cambridge Ritualist school that transformed classical studies.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #17087 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/17087)

andersens-fairy-tales.name: Andersen's Fairy Tales
andersens-fairy-tales.description:
Eighteen fairy tales by Hans Christian Andersen (1805-1875), the Danish master whose stories transformed children's literature forever. From the naked Emperor to the freezing match girl, from the Snow Queen's icy palace to the red shoes that would not stop dancing, these tales blend wonder, humor, and an unflinching honesty about suffering that sets Andersen apart from all other fairy tale writers. Unlike the Grimms, Andersen wrote original literary fairy tales drawn from his own imagination and his own pain — and they are among the most widely translated works in all of literature. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1597 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1597).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Vilhelm Pedersen's original illustrations for Andersen's tales (1849-1850s) — the first and most iconic illustrations, pen-and-ink, public domain. Lorenz Frølich's illustrations (1870s) — refined pen-and-ink drawings, public domain. Arthur Rackham's illustrations for 'Fairy Tales by Hans Andersen' (1932, Harrap) — watercolor and pen-and-ink in Rackham's distinctive style, public domain. Edmund Dulac's illustrations for 'Stories from Hans Andersen' (1911, Hodder & Stoughton) — luminous color plates, public domain. W. Heath Robinson's illustrations for 'Hans Andersen's Fairy Tales' (1913, Constable) — pen-and-ink with gentle humor, public domain. Kay Nielsen's illustrations for 'Fairy Tales by Hans Andersen' (1924, Hodder & Stoughton) — Art Nouveau masterworks, public domain.

animal-fables.name: Animal Fables: The Stories Nobody Finished
animal-fables.description:
Twenty-five one-minute fables with no morals. Each story: one animal, one impossible situation, one silence at the end. Structurally lifted from the world's great epics — Mahabharata, Ramayana, Odyssey, Gilgamesh, Norse myth, Greek tragedy, Jataka tales — but completely standalone. A kid never needs to know it's the Mahabharata. Pull one card at breakfast. Talk about it or don't.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's animal illustrations from Aesop's Fables (1912, Heinemann) — ink and watercolor, expressive animal faces with human weight behind the eyes. Walter Crane's picture book animals (1887, Routledge) — bold outlines, decorative borders, animals that feel like they're thinking. Milo Winter's Aesop illustrations (1919, Rand McNally) — naturalistic animals in fable situations, warm palette. Gustave Doré's La Fontaine Fables (1868, Hachette) — dramatic chiaroscuro engravings, animals with gravity.

anne-of-green-gables.name: Anne of Green Gables
anne-of-green-gables.description:
L.M. Montgomery's Anne of Green Gables (1908) — the story of Anne Shirley, a fierce, imaginative orphan who transforms the quiet community of Avonlea, Prince Edward Island, through the sheer force of her personality, her love of beauty, and her hunger for belonging. From the train station to the bend in the road, thirty-eight chapters trace Anne's journey from unwanted child to beloved daughter, exploring imagination, resilience, friendship, and the quiet power of love.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #45 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/45).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- M.A. and W.A.J. Claus (1908, L.C. Page & Company) — original edition illustrators, black-and-white plates capturing Anne's arrival and Avonlea life
- Elizabeth Chicken (1933, Harrap) — beloved British edition illustrations with soft, pastoral watercolors of Prince Edward Island
- Sybil Tawse (1933, Pitman) — delicate pen-and-ink drawings

anon-shiryo-cat-stories.name: Anon's Robot Stories: Shiryo & Bishisheshi
anon-shiryo-cat-stories.description: A collection of children's stories where Anon — a genderless, ageless parent learning through life — watches their robot Shiryo learn to care for a kitten named Bishisheshi. Each story teaches a relational grammar: a pattern of how to be with others and with nature. Designed for recursive AI-assisted creation — each story cover acts as a generative seed containing enough context (values, characters, visual references, symbolic roots) for an AI collaborator to generate missing images and text while maintaining the soul of the story.

anthropological-dance.name: Anthropological Dance: The Body Politic
anthropological-dance.description:
Every culture dances. This grammar maps dance traditions from six continents through the lens of function — why do people dance? Sacred ritual, social bonding, martial power, courtship, resistance, trance. From Sufi whirling to Afrobeat, from Balinese temple dance to New York voguing, each tradition encodes a culture's relationship to the body, to power, to the divine. Dance is the first language; this grammar is its dictionary.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Henri Matisse, *La Danse* (1910). Edgar Degas, dance studies and pastels. Ancient Egyptian dance reliefs (Tomb of Nebamun, ~1350 BCE). Mughal miniature paintings of court dancers. Greek red-figure vase paintings of maenads and satyrs.

arabian-nights.name: The Arabian Nights Entertainments
arabian-nights.description:
The Thousand and One Nights — one of the greatest story collections in world literature — told through Andrew Lang's 1898 selection. Thirty-four tales organized into eight story cycles, from the frame story of Scheherazade's life-saving narration through the voyages of Sindbad, the wonders of Aladdin, and the justice of Haroun-al-Raschid. Each story exists because a life depends on its telling.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #128 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/128). Andrew Lang's edition, after Longmans, Green and Co, 1918 (1898).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Edmund Dulac's illustrations for Stories from the Arabian Nights (1907) — the definitive visual companion. Maxfield Parrish's Arabian Nights illustrations (1909). René Bull's illustrations for the Newnes edition. William Harvey's woodcuts for Lane's translation (1841). Persian and Mughal miniature paintings depicting scenes from Alf Layla wa-Layla.

archetypal-astrology-tarnas.name: Archetypal Astrology — Richard Tarnas
archetypal-astrology-tarnas.description:
A grammar of planetary archetypes based on Richard Tarnas's archetypal astrology, as developed in 'Cosmos and Psyche' (2006) and his lectures at the California Institute of Integral Studies. Unlike traditional astrology, Tarnas's framework centers on the PLANETS as primary archetypal forces — not the zodiac signs. Each planet embodies a multivalent archetype drawn from Greek mythology, depth psychology, and the history of ideas. The power of the system lies in planetary ASPECTS — when two or more planetary archetypes combine (through conjunction, opposition, square, or trine), they produce emergent archetypal dynamics that can be observed in both individual psychology and collective cultural events.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Classical paintings of the planetary deities: Botticelli's 'Birth of Venus', Caravaggio's 'Jupiter, Neptune, and Pluto', Velázquez's 'Mars', Rubens's 'Saturn Devouring His Son', Raphael's 'The School of Athens' (Mercury/Hermes). Giovanni Battista Tiepolo's ceiling frescoes of planetary gods (18th century). Ancient Roman frescoes and mosaics depicting planetary deities (Pompeii, Herculaneum). Medieval and Renaissance astronomical manuscripts with planetary symbols and orbits. William Blake's mythological paintings (Uranus/creative rebellion imagery). Gustave Moreau's symbolist paintings of mythological figures (Jupiter, Prometheus). NASA planetary photographs for the outer planets (public domain).

architecture-of-nations.name: The Architecture of Nations
architecture-of-nations.description:
A structural survey of how the modern world was built — the treaties that drew borders, the policies that standardized languages, the frameworks that defined who counts as a nation and who doesn't, and the knowledge systems that persist beneath and beyond those structures. 45 cards across six domains: Borders & Maps, Languages & Scripts, Sovereignty & Recognition, Development & Progress, Knowledge & Patrimony, and Resistance & Persistence.

Each card presents what happened, the underlying pattern, and what still operates today. No editorializing needed — the architecture speaks for itself.

areopagitica.name: Areopagitica
areopagitica.description:
John Milton's Areopagitica (1644) -- the foundational argument for freedom of the press. Written as an unlicensed pamphlet defying the very law it opposed, it argues against Parliament's Licensing Order with a force that echoes across four centuries. Milton marshals history (censorship was invented by the Inquisition), theology (virtue requires the knowledge of evil), and practical sense (licensing is impossible to enforce) into the most eloquent case ever made for the free circulation of ideas. 'Who kills a man kills a reasonable creature, God's image; but he who destroys a good book, kills reason itself.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #608 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/608)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 17th century English Civil War engravings. William Faithorne's portrait of Milton. Title pages of original 1644 pamphlet editions.

art-of-love-ovid.name: The Art of Love (Ars Amatoria)
art-of-love-ovid.description:
Ovid's scandalous and brilliant verse manual on the art of love, in three books: finding love, keeping love, and a woman's guide to love. Written around 2 CE, it earned Ovid exile from Rome but became one of the most influential poems in Western literature. This prose translation by Henry T. Riley preserves the wit and structure of the original.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #47677 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/47677)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John William Waterhouse's classical paintings of Roman love scenes (1890s-1900s); Sir Lawrence Alma-Tadema's paintings of Roman daily life and leisure (1870s-1900s); illustrations from 18th-century French editions of the Ars Amatoria.

art-of-war.name: The Art of War — Sun Tzu
art-of-war.description:
The foundational text on strategy and warfare by Sun Tzu (544-496 BCE), Chinese military general, strategist, and philosopher. Thirteen chapters covering strategic planning, tactical dispositions, energy, terrain, espionage, and the art of winning without fighting. 'Supreme excellence consists in breaking the enemy's resistance without fighting.' Lionel Giles translation (1910), the definitive English edition. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #132 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/132). Commentary and footnotes by Giles have been stripped — only Sun Tzu's original verses are included.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese calligraphy and brush paintings of the Art of War text. Bamboo slip manuscripts — the earliest surviving copies of Sun Tzu were written on bamboo strips (Yinqueshan Han Slips, 2nd century BCE). Chinese landscape paintings from the Song and Ming dynasties depicting military campaigns. Illustrations from early printed editions of the Art of War (Chinese woodblock prints). Japanese ukiyo-e prints depicting military strategy (Utagawa school). European military maps and battle diagrams from 18th-19th century editions.

art-with-mati-and-dada.name: Art with Mati and Dada
art-with-mati-and-dada.description: A delightful journey through art history as Mati & Dada meet famous painters across time. Fun stories, gentle pacing, and beautiful visuals introduce kids to great artists in a way that’s engaging, educational, and safe for young viewers

ballet-history-tree.name: Ballet History Tree
ballet-history-tree.description:
A decolonial map of classical ballet — 13 landmark ballets traced from the sanitized stage back to their original stories, the traditions they silenced or borrowed from, and the movements they birthed. Each ballet is a node in a tree with three root systems: the choreographic innovation it brought to dance, the darker or richer original narrative it softened, and the living traditions it caricatured or could be in dialogue with. For every classic, a kid-friendly countercultural dance or theater piece is offered as a companion — something that breaks the frame while keeping the wonder.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Edgar Degas ballet paintings and pastels (1870s-1890s) — rehearsal rooms, stage scenes, dancers at the barre, public domain. Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec dance lithographs (1890s) — theatrical posters and backstage scenes, public domain. Léon Bakst costume and set designs for Ballets Russes (1909-1921) — vivid orientalist designs for Firebird, Scheherazade, and others, public domain. Alexandre Benois set designs for Petrouchka and other Diaghilev productions (1910s), public domain. Valentin Serov's portrait of Ida Rubinstein and Anna Pavlova sketches, public domain.

ballet-living-architecture.name: Ballet: A Living Architecture
ballet-living-architecture.description:
Ballet is architecture in motion — a 400-year tradition of codified positions, geometries of the body, and gravity-defying feats that evolved from courtly entertainment to one of humanity's most demanding art forms. This grammar maps the vocabulary (positions, steps, jumps), the canon (Swan Lake to The Rite of Spring), and the eras (court ballet to contemporary) — always asking what the body is being asked to do, and why.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Edgar Degas, ballet paintings and pastels (1870s-1890s). Engravings from Raoul-Auger Feuillet's *Chorégraphie* (1700). Lithographs of Marie Taglioni and Romantic era ballerinas. Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec, dance-hall paintings.

beyond-good-and-evil.name: Beyond Good and Evil
beyond-good-and-evil.description:
Friedrich Nietzsche's Beyond Good and Evil: Prelude to a Philosophy of the Future (1886) — his most systematic revaluation of Western philosophy and morality. Nine chapters and a preface, mixing sustained argument with brilliant aphorism, attacking the prejudices of philosophers, analyzing the psychology of religion and morality, and introducing the master-slave morality distinction. Translated by Helen Zimmern, whose work was supervised by Nietzsche himself.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #4363 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/4363)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of Nietzsche (various, 1860s-1880s), including the famous 1882 portrait by Gustav Schultze. Images of Sils-Maria in the Upper Engadine where Nietzsche wrote. Edvard Munch's portrait of Nietzsche (1906). Title pages from early editions.

bhagavad-gita.name: The Bhagavad Gita (Song Celestial)
bhagavad-gita.description:
The Bhagavad Gita ('Song of God'), translated as 'The Song Celestial' by Sir Edwin Arnold (1885). A 700-verse discourse between Prince Arjuna and the god Krishna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, embedded within the Mahabharata epic. In 18 chapters, Krishna reveals the paths of knowledge, action, meditation, and devotion, culminating in the vision of his cosmic form and the teaching of ultimate surrender. The most widely read and influential text in Hindu philosophy.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2388 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2388)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Indian miniature paintings of the Bhagavad Gita (Rajasthani and Pahari schools, 17th-19th century), particularly the Kurukshetra battle scenes and Krishna revealing his Vishwarupa form. Also: illustrations from Edwin Arnold's 'The Light of Asia' and Victorian-era Orientalist depictions of the Mahabharata.

biblical-stories-kids.name: Stories from the Living Stars
biblical-stories-kids.description:
Biblical tales retold as cosmic mythology for bedtime. 48 stories in rhyming verse with an esoteric, animist lens — Jesus as shaman for the Cosmic Christ, angels as star-beings, nature as conscious participant. Designed for 5-minute bedtime reading with karaoke-style TTS and full-bleed illustrations.

Source: Original retellings from memory, with story details cross-referenced against the World English Bible (Project Gutenberg eBook #8294, https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/8294).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Gustave Doré's Bible illustrations (La Grande Bible de Tours, 1866) — 241 dramatic engravings with cosmic scale and chiaroscuro lighting, ideal primary illustrations. Julius Schnorr von Carolsfeld's Die Bibel in Bildern (1860) — 240 clear woodcuts for supplementary coverage. James Tissot's watercolors (Brooklyn Museum, 1886-1902) — full color biblical scenes for variety. William Blake's visionary paintings (1795-1827) — perfect for cosmic/esoteric items (seraphim, Ezekiel's wheels, Jacob's ladder).

biblical-stories-kids-storyboard.name: Stories from the Living Stars — Storyboard
biblical-stories-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on Stories from the Living Stars. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

book-of-tea.name: The Book of Tea
book-of-tea.description:
The Book of Tea by Kakuzo Okakura (1906), a luminous essay on the Japanese tea ceremony as a pathway to understanding Eastern aesthetics, philosophy, and the art of living. In seven chapters, Okakura traces the roots of Teaism through Taoism and Zen Buddhism, explores the architecture of the tea-room, the appreciation of art and flowers, and the lives of the great tea-masters -- culminating in the unforgettable story of Rikiu's last tea ceremony. Written in English for a Western audience, it remains one of the most beautiful bridges between Eastern and Western thought.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #769 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/769)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Japanese woodblock prints (ukiyo-e) of tea ceremonies by Kitagawa Utamaro, Suzuki Harunobu, and Utagawa Hiroshige. Also: photographs of traditional tea-rooms and chashitsu architecture. Illustrations from early 20th century editions of the book.

book-of-the-dead-egyptian.name: The Book of the Dead
book-of-the-dead-egyptian.description:
E.A. Wallis Budge's introduction to the Egyptian Book of the Dead, the great funerary text known to the ancient Egyptians as 'Per-t em Hru' (Coming Forth by Day). This work explains the origins, authorship, and contents of the spells, hymns, and magical formulae that guided the dead through the Underworld to the judgment hall of Osiris and beyond to the blessed fields of the afterlife. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #7145 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/7145)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The Papyrus of Ani (British Museum), illustrated with scenes of the Weighing of the Heart, the Fields of the Blessed, and the gods of the Underworld. Also: the Book of the Dead papyri reproductions by Edouard Naville (1886) and Karl Richard Lepsius (1842), both featuring hieroglyphic vignettes in the traditional Egyptian style.

bulfinch-s-classical-mythology.name: Bulfinch's Classical Mythology
bulfinch-s-classical-mythology.description: Mythological stories of the gods, heroes, and constellations from Thomas Bulfinch's 'The Age of Fable' (1855). Presents the Greek and Roman myths behind the planets and zodiac signs, connecting astrology to its ancient narrative roots.

bulfinch-s-classical-mythology-202601272.name: Bulfinch's Classical Mythology
bulfinch-s-classical-mythology-202601272.description: Mythological stories of the gods, heroes, and constellations from Thomas Bulfinch's 'The Age of Fable' (1855). Presents the Greek and Roman myths behind the planets and zodiac signs, connecting astrology to its ancient narrative roots.

bus-grammar-system-prompts.name: Bus Grammar — System Prompts for Passengers
bus-grammar-system-prompts.description:
32 system prompts for AI voice passengers across 6 therapeutic and archetypal 'buses': Facing Human Wreckage (Vanessa Andreotti's Hospicing Modernity), Ancestral Voices (indigenous/ecological epistemology), Jungian Archetypes (depth psychology), IFS Parts (Internal Family Systems), Chakra Voices (Hindu/yogic energy system), and DBT Mind States (Dialectical Behavior Therapy). Each prompt is designed to interpret oracle draws (tarot, I Ching, etc.) through its tradition's lens. Includes 2 Facilitator voices for synthesis across buses.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Alchemical illustrations from 'Rosarium Philosophorum' (1550) and 'Atalanta Fugiens' by Michael Maier (1617) for Jungian voices. William Blake's mythological engravings (1790s-1820s) for archetypal depth. Hindu temple sculpture and tantric lotus motifs from Mughal miniatures for Chakra voices. Hokusai's 'Great Wave' and Zen enso circles for DBT mind states. Lascaux and Altamira cave paintings for Ancestral voices. Giovanni Battista Piranesi's 'Carceri d'Invenzione' (Imaginary Prisons, 1745-1761) for FHW voices — metaphor for modernity's invisible structures.

celtic-fairy-tales.name: Celtic Fairy Tales
celtic-fairy-tales.description:
Twenty-six fairy tales from Irish, Scottish, and Welsh tradition, selected and edited by Joseph Jacobs (1892) with illustrations by John D. Batten. This landmark anthology gathers tales of fairy enchantment, heroic quests, comic trickery, tragic love, and legendary giants from across the Celtic world — from the ancient Mabinogion and the Ulster Cycle to the living folk tradition of the fireside seanchai. Includes the great tales of Deirdre of the Sorrows, the Wooing of Olwen, Finn McCool at Knockmany, and the Irish Cinderella. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #7885 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/7885).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John D. Batten's original illustrations for the 1892 David Nutt edition — striking line drawings and color plates in Arts and Crafts style depicting scenes from each tale, including ornamental chapter headings and full-page illustrations of fairy maidens, giants, and Celtic heroes. Batten's work for both Celtic Fairy Tales and its companion volume More Celtic Fairy Tales (1894) represents some of the finest fairy tale illustration of the Victorian era.

children-story-tree.name: The Story Tree: Children's Stories for Every Age
children-story-tree.description:
A curated collection of 33 essential children's stories drawn from the world's great traditions — Aesop's fables, Grimm and Andersen fairy tales, Greek and Norse myths, folk tales from six continents, and beloved classics of children's literature. Each story is told four ways: a gentle preschool version for the youngest listeners, a faithful classic telling, cultural roots showing how the story travels across traditions, and a modern reflection on why it still matters. Cross-linked to full source grammars in this repository.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's fairy tale illustrations (1900s-1920s) — atmospheric watercolors for Grimm, Andersen, and Aesop. Walter Crane's picture books (1870s-1890s) — Art Nouveau nursery illustrations. Edmund Dulac's fairy tale plates (1907-1916) — rich color illustrations for Arabian Nights, Andersen, and myth collections. Gustave Doré's fairy tale engravings (1862-1870s) — dramatic black-and-white for Perrault and La Fontaine. John Tenniel's Alice illustrations (1865) — the definitive Alice in Wonderland images. W.W. Denslow's Oz illustrations (1900) — original Wizard of Oz art. Randolph Caldecott's nursery illustrations (1878-1886) — lively watercolors of folk tales and nursery rhymes.

chinese-literature-anthology.name: Chinese Literature Anthology
chinese-literature-anthology.description:
A comprehensive anthology of Chinese literature compiled by Epiphanius Wilson (1900), comprising five major works spanning over a thousand years: The Analects of Confucius (translated by William Jennings), The Sayings of Mencius (translated by James Legge), The Shi-King/Book of Poetry (translated by Sir John Francis Davis), The Travels of Fa-Hien (translated by James Legge), and The Sorrows of Han (translated by Sir John Francis Davis).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #10056 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/10056)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Chinese scroll paintings, ink wash landscapes, and court scenes from the Ming and Qing dynasties would complement these texts. Illustrations from early 20th century editions of Chinese classics published by Colonial Press (1900) are also relevant.

chuang-tzu.name: Chuang Tzu: Mystic, Moralist, and Social Reformer
chuang-tzu.description:
The complete Chuang Tzu (Zhuangzi) in Herbert A. Giles' landmark 1889 translation — all 33 chapters of one of the foundational texts of Taoist philosophy. Chuang Tzu (c. 369-286 BCE) was the most brilliant and creative of the ancient Chinese philosophers, whose parables of the butterfly dream, the cook and the ox, and the useless tree have shaped world thought for over two millennia. The Inner Chapters (1-7) are attributed to Chuang Tzu himself; the Outer (8-22) and Miscellaneous (23-33) chapters were composed by his followers and editors.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #59709 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/59709)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese ink wash paintings (shuimo hua) depicting Taoist themes — particularly works from the Song Dynasty (960-1279) such as those by Liang Kai and Mu Qi, whose spare, spontaneous brushwork embodies the Taoist aesthetic. Also: illustrations from early printed editions of the Zhuangzi, and the tradition of literati painting (wenren hua) that drew deeply from Chuang Tzu's nature philosophy.

civil-disobedience.name: Civil Disobedience
civil-disobedience.description:
Henry David Thoreau's 'Civil Disobedience' (1849, originally 'Resistance to Civil Government') — the foundational text of nonviolent resistance. Written after Thoreau spent a night in jail for refusing to pay a poll tax that supported slavery and the Mexican-American War. This essay directly influenced Gandhi's satyagraha, Martin Luther King Jr.'s civil rights strategy, and every nonviolent resistance movement since. 'Under a government which imprisons any unjustly, the true place for a just man is also a prison.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #71 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/71)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Daguerreotypes of Thoreau. Illustrations from early editions of Walden. Concord, Massachusetts landscapes by the Hudson River School painters.

colonial-decolonial-music-spiral.name: The Music Spiral: Colonial & Decolonial Voices in Dialogue
colonial-decolonial-music-spiral.description:
Music has always been both weapon and wound. Colonial powers extracted rhythms, flattened traditions into spectacle, and sold back sanitized versions of what they stole. But the stolen music never stopped moving — it spiraled back, transformed, decolonized, and often consumed the colonizer's culture from within. This grammar maps that spiral: each turn pairs a colonial voice with a decolonial voice, not as good vs. evil but as a living conversation. Vinicius de Moraes dreams in bossa nova while Gilberto Gil detonates it with Tropicália. Carmen Miranda is packaged as a banana-hat export while Belafonte turns the banana boat into a workers' anthem. Taylor Swift perfects the pop machine while Beyoncé rebuilds it as a Black cathedral. The spiral never stops turning.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Concert and music posters from the WPA Federal Art Project (1930s-40s). African musical instrument illustrations from 19th-century ethnographic surveys. Sheet music cover art from Tin Pan Alley era. Brazilian carnival photography (early 20th century, public domain collections).

colonial-tongues.name: Línguas Coloniais / Colonial Tongues: The Violence We Speak Without Knowing
colonial-tongues.description:
Every language carries its history in its idioms. In Portuguese: 'judiar' means to mistreat — from 'judeu' (Jew). 'Denegrir' means to defame — from 'negro' (black). A nightstand is a 'criado-mudo' — a mute servant. In English: 'gypped' comes from 'Gypsy.' 'Sold down the river' was literal slave trading. Children sing 'Eeny Meeny Miny Moe' not knowing it once used the n-word. This grammar maps the colonial, antisemitic, racist, classist, and religious violence embedded in everyday speech in both Portuguese and English, with mutual translations that reveal how the same structures of oppression speak through different mouths. Includes children's songs — because colonialism's deepest move is to get into the lullaby.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura). 19th-century children's book illustrations (Kate Greenaway, Walter Crane). Portuguese colonial-era broadsheets. WPA poster art. Brazilian folk art prints.

common-sense.name: Common Sense
common-sense.description:
Thomas Paine's Common Sense (1776) -- the pamphlet that made the American Revolution inevitable. In plain language that any colonist could understand, Paine argued that hereditary monarchy was absurd, that reconciliation with Britain was impossible, and that the time for independence was NOW. It sold 500,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million. 'A long habit of not thinking a thing wrong, gives it a superficial appearance of being right.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #147 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/147)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 18th century American Revolutionary War engravings. Paul Revere's prints. John Trumbull's Declaration of Independence paintings.

communist-manifesto.name: The Communist Manifesto
communist-manifesto.description:
The Communist Manifesto (1848) by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels — the most influential political pamphlet in history. In four chapters, it argues that all history is the history of class struggle, analyzes the revolutionary role of the bourgeoisie, and calls for the abolition of private property. Placed on the 'contested' shelf: it is both a brilliant analysis of capitalism's dynamics AND a blueprint that was used to justify immense suffering. Both truths coexist.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #61 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/61)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 19th century political cartoons and broadsheets. Honoré Daumier's lithographs of workers. Gustave Doré's illustrations of London poverty. Soviet constructivist posters (early period, pre-Stalin).

confucian-analects.name: The Confucian Analects
confucian-analects.description: The collected sayings and teachings of Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551-479 BC), compiled by his disciples. Level 1 items contain the original Legge translation text of each individual teaching. Level 2 items are the 20 traditional books with thematic summaries.

consciousness-of-the-atom.name: Consciousness of the Atom
consciousness-of-the-atom.description:
Alice Bailey's 1922 lecture series tracing consciousness from the atom to the solar system. Seven lectures build a single argument: that every form in nature — from the smallest atom to the greatest star — is a living, conscious entity at some stage of evolutionary unfoldment. This grammar extracts the factual propositions about reality (not just the seven rays) and presents them as contemplation cards.

Source: Alice A. Bailey, *The Consciousness of the Atom*, Lucifer Publishing Co., 1922. Public domain (pre-1929 US publication).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Sacred geometry and atomic/cosmic diagrams from early 20th century theosophical publications. Annie Besant and C.W. Leadbeater's *Occult Chemistry* (1908) contains relevant atomic clairvoyant diagrams. Ernst Haeckel's *Art Forms in Nature* (1904) provides beautiful biological form illustrations that echo the atom-to-organism consciousness thread.

cosmic-unfolding.name: Cosmic Unfolding
cosmic-unfolding.description:
The story of everything — from the Big Bang to you reading this sentence. Thirty milestones in the 13.8-billion-year history of the universe, told with wonder and awe. Each event is a turning point: the moment the first light escaped, the first star ignited, the first planet formed, the first cell divided, the first eye opened. Tiered content for young children (Story) and older readers (Science).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Camille Flammarion (1842–1925), whose astronomical illustrations in 'Astronomie Populaire' (1880) and 'L'Atmosphère' (1888) combined scientific accuracy with cosmic wonder. Étienne Léopold Trouvelot (1827–1895), whose astronomical pastels at Harvard Observatory captured planets, nebulae, and solar phenomena with remarkable beauty.

creative-evolution.name: Creative Evolution
creative-evolution.description:
Henri Bergson's Creative Evolution (L'Évolution créatrice, 1907) — the work that introduced élan vital into modern thought and won Bergson the Nobel Prize in Literature. Against both mechanism and teleology, Bergson argues that life is driven by a vital impetus that creates unpredictably as it flows through matter. Four chapters trace this impetus through the divergence of plant, insect, and human evolution, culminating in a brilliant critique of Western philosophy's inability to think real becoming. Translated by Arthur Mitchell (1911).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #26163 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/26163)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of Henri Bergson (early 1900s). Diagrams of evolutionary divergence from early 20th century biology texts. Ernst Haeckel's tree of life illustrations from Generelle Morphologie (1866). Early cinema apparatus illustrations showing the cinematograph mechanism Bergson uses as his central metaphor.

critique-pure-reason.name: Critique of Pure Reason
critique-pure-reason.description:
Immanuel Kant's Critique of Pure Reason (Kritik der reinen Vernunft, 1781; second edition 1787) — the founding text of critical philosophy and one of the most important works in the history of Western thought. Kant's 'Copernican revolution' argues that the mind actively structures experience through forms of sensibility and categories of understanding, making science possible but setting limits on metaphysical knowledge. Fourteen major divisions cover the Transcendental Aesthetic (space and time), Analytic (categories and principles), Dialectic (illusions about soul, cosmos, and God), and Doctrine of Method. Translated by J. M. D. Meiklejohn.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #4280 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/4280)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Portraits of Immanuel Kant, including the famous engraving after the painting by Döbler (1791). Views of Königsberg (now Kaliningrad). Title pages of the first and second editions (1781, 1787). Diagrams from 19th-century commentaries illustrating the table of categories and the architectonic of pure reason.

darkwater.name: Darkwater: Voices from Within the Veil
darkwater.description:
W. E. B. Du Bois's 'Darkwater: Voices from Within the Veil' (1920) — the fiery sequel to 'The Souls of Black Folk.' Where Souls was measured and sociological, Darkwater is passionate and prophetic. Ten essays on race, labor, democracy, feminism, and empire are interwoven with poems, prayers, allegorical stories, and one remarkable science fiction tale. Du Bois analyzes whiteness as a global system of exploitation, argues for women's suffrage and workers' rights, and imagines a world transformed. Contains 'The Souls of White Folk,' 'A Litany at Atlanta,' and 'The Comet.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #15210 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/15210)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of W. E. B. Du Bois circa 1920. Illustrations from the 1920 first edition published by Harcourt, Brace and Howe. Photographs from the Crisis magazine. Images from the 1900 Paris Exposition Negro Exhibit.

dbt-wise-heart.name: The Wise Heart: DBT Skills Through World Myths
dbt-wise-heart.description:
Every skill a child needs to handle big feelings, told through the world's oldest stories. This grammar maps all four modules of Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) — Mindfulness, Distress Tolerance, Emotional Regulation, and Interpersonal Effectiveness — onto public domain myths from multiple traditions. Each story teaches one specific skill through narrative, not lecture, following the Daniel Tiger pattern: name the skill, embed it in a relatable story, give it a singable mantra, no shame. The editorial stance is explicit: we are building the society we want to live in.

Designed as a companion to the Shiryo & Bishisheshi stories — a robot, a kitten, and eventually Alice herself will visit these myths and practice what they learn.

decolonization-five-domains.name: Decolonization: Five Domains
decolonization-five-domains.description:
A contemplative grammar mapping decolonization across five domains of human experience — Body, Mind, Spirit, Collective, and Planet. Not a checklist or a destination, but an ongoing practice of noticing: who told you how to eat, think, pray, organize, and relate to the earth? Each item traces a colonial pattern, names what it costs, honors what persists despite centuries of erasure, and extends an invitation to sit with complexity rather than rush toward resolution.

This grammar follows Vanessa Andreotti's insight that modernity is dying and needs hospicing — not with triumphalism or despair, but with the wisdom of grandmothers who know that composting is not the same as destroying.

decolonizing-childhood.name: Decolonizing Childhood — The Spirits Speak
decolonizing-childhood.description:
A grammar of childhood told by three lines of Umbanda spirits working together: Erês (child spirits who know from inside), Caboclos (indigenous ancestors who remember how children were raised on the land), and Pretos Velhos (elder Black spirits who carried wisdom through the worst and kept singing). Each item addresses a developmental threshold — birth, naming, play, fear, sexuality, death, passage — and maps the correspondence between individual development and cultural structure: every inner stage has its communal mirror, and when colonialism broke the mirror, the stages didn't disappear — they just lost their witness.

This is not a critique. Children really do develop in stages. Indigenous peoples knew this — that's why they had rites of passage. The colonial move wasn't discovering childhood; it was severing the correspondence between inner growth and communal recognition, then selling the gap back as products and institutions.

Grounded in Charles Eastman (Ohiyesa)'s Lakota childhood accounts, Zitkala-Ša's boarding school testimonies, Winnicott's developmental insights, and Gayle Rubin's macro/micro framework. The Erês of Umbanda — Cosme, Damião, and their lines — are not lesser spirits but carriers of a specific wisdom: joy, spontaneity, and truth-telling that adults have lost access to.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur C. Parker's illustrations from Skunny Wundy: Seneca Indian Tales (1926), Zitkala-Ša's own illustrations from Old Indian Legends (1901), Edward Curtis photographs of indigenous childhood and ceremony (pre-1927 publications), Frances Densmore's photographs of Ojibwe and Dakota childhood practices.

dhammapada.name: The Dhammapada
dhammapada.description: The Dhammapada ('Path of Dhamma'), a collection of 423 verses attributed to the Buddha, is one of the most widely read Buddhist scriptures. Level 1 items contain individual verses from the F. Max Muller translation (1881). Level 2 items are the 26 traditional chapters with thematic summaries.

dinosaur-tree.name: Dinosaur Tree
dinosaur-tree.description:
A journey through the age of dinosaurs — from the first tiny hunters of the Triassic to the mighty giants of the Cretaceous. Thirty species organized by the three great periods of the Mesozoic Era, with tiered content for young children (Story) and older readers (Science).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Charles R. Knight (1874–1953), whose paintings for the American Museum of Natural History defined how generations imagined dinosaurs. Heinrich Harder (1858–1935), German artist known for his prehistoric animal paintings. Zdeněk Burian (1905–1981), Czech paleoartist whose reconstructions shaped European understanding of prehistory.

diogenes-laertius.name: Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers
diogenes-laertius.description:
Diogenes Laertius's compendium of ancient Greek philosophy — biographical sketches, doctrines, sayings, and anecdotes of philosophers from Thales to Epicurus, spanning seven centuries of thought. Translated by C.D. Yonge (1915).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #57342 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/57342)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Thomas Stanley's 'History of Philosophy' (1655-1662) contains engraved portraits of ancient philosophers. The Nuremberg Chronicle (1493) includes woodcut portraits of philosophers.

discourses-epictetus.name: Discourses of Epictetus with the Encheiridion
discourses-epictetus.description:
A selection from the Discourses of Epictetus (c. 50-135 CE), the Stoic philosopher who was born a slave and became one of the most influential teachers of practical ethics in the ancient world. Includes the complete Encheiridion (Manual), a distillation of his core teachings. Translated by George Long (1877). "It is not things that disturb us, but our judgments about things." Epictetus taught that freedom lies not in controlling circumstances but in mastering our responses to them — a teaching that directly influenced Marcus Aurelius, and centuries later, cognitive behavioral therapy. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #10661 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/10661).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Engraved portrait of Epictetus from Thomas Stanley's "History of Philosophy" (1655). Illustrations from 17th and 18th century editions of the Encheiridion. Classical Greek and Roman sculpture photography — busts of Stoic philosophers. Manuscript illuminations from medieval copies of the Discourses. Engravings of Nicopolis ruins where Epictetus taught. Illustrations from Edward Poynter and Lawrence Alma-Tadema depicting Roman philosophical life.

disney-decolonial-tree.name: Disney Decolonial Tree
disney-decolonial-tree.description:
A living map of Disney's animated canon split into two root systems: the composting pile (films, scenes, and myths that encode colonial logic and are dying — or need to die — with honesty) and the thriving canopy (films where something genuinely new is growing from indigenous, diasporic, or non-Western soil). Each film is traced back to its original mythos — the real Pocahontas, the actual Polynesian wayfinding tradition, the Sámi reindeer herders Frozen 2 quietly honored — and forward to what Disney did with it. The Romantic Myth of True Love gets its own composting ceremony. Not a cancellation list. A forest inventory: what's rotting, what's sprouting, and what nutrients the rot is releasing into the soil.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Kay Nielsen's illustrations for 'East of the Sun, West of the Moon' (1914) and his Disney concept art (much now in public domain). Edmund Dulac's fairy tale illustrations (1910s) — orientalist but extraordinarily beautiful. Arthur Rackham's fairy tale work. Warwick Goble's Indian fairy tale illustrations (1912). Gustave Doré's fairy tale engravings. John Bauer's Scandinavian troll and forest illustrations (1907-1915) — directly relevant to Frozen's Nordic roots.

disney-dreams-the-original-selves.name: Disney Dreams: The Original Selves
disney-dreams-the-original-selves.description:
What happens when Disney characters fall asleep and meet the REAL people they were based on? Eight silly, respectful, rhyming dream-stories for five-year-olds in which Ariel meets the girl who became sea foam, Pocahontas meets the real Amonute who was only twelve, Sleeping Beauty meets a Viking warrior woman, and Aladdin meets the Syrian man who actually made up his story. Each dream is a gentle introduction to the richer, stranger, older versions of the stories kids already love — told in verse, with proper attribution to the original sources, and just enough silliness to keep a kindergartener giggling.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Vilhelm Pedersen's original illustrations for Hans Christian Andersen's fairy tales (1849) — the first and most authentic visual interpretations, public domain. Kay Nielsen's 'East of the Sun, West of the Moon' (1914) — luminous art nouveau fairy tale illustrations. Arthur Rackham's fairy tale work (1900s-1930s). Edmund Dulac's Arabian Nights illustrations (1907). Warwick Goble's 'Indian Myth and Legend' illustrations (1913). John Bauer's Scandinavian troll and forest illustrations (1907-1915).

dreamtime-kids.name: The Songlines: Stories from the Dreaming
dreamtime-kids.description:
A bedtime mythology grammar drawing from the oldest continuous spiritual tradition on Earth — Aboriginal Australian Dreamtime stories. Forty-two rhyming verses retell publicly shared stories of how Ancestor Spirits sang the land into existence, how the Rainbow Serpent carved the rivers, how the Emu walks the Milky Way, and how Songlines map a continent in song.

CULTURAL SENSITIVITY NOTE: Aboriginal Dreamtime stories carry deep cultural protocols. Some stories are sacred and restricted — shared only in ceremony, only with initiated people, only on particular Country. This grammar uses ONLY stories that Aboriginal storytellers and elders have themselves shared publicly through authorized retellings, published children's books, and educational programs. We name specific nations where possible. We acknowledge that excluding Aboriginal voices from a global mythology series would itself be a colonial act — erasing the oldest continuous culture on Earth from a collection that includes younger traditions. But inclusion must be done with respect: we share what has been shared with us, nothing more.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Rock art photographs from Kakadu National Park and Kimberley region (Creative Commons licensed, Australian Government archives). Aboriginal bark paintings from the National Gallery of Australia open-access collection (artists including Narritjin Maymuru, Yirawala, and John Mawurndjul — always credited by name and nation). Contemporary Aboriginal children's book illustrations by artists such as Bronwyn Bancroft (Djanbun clan), Julie Dowling (Badimaya), and Sally Morgan (Palku/Nhanda) — used only with proper attribution to artist and nation. Photographs of Country (landscape features that ARE the stories) from Creative Commons sources.

dreamtime-kids-storyboard.name: The Songlines: Stories from the Dreaming — Storyboard
dreamtime-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on The Songlines: Stories from the Dreaming. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

economic-ideas.name: The Grammar of Economic Ideas
economic-ideas.description:
Fifty-five foundational economic concepts — from Adam Smith to Karl Marx to Elinor Ostrom to Kropotkin — presented without hierarchy between schools of thought. Classical, behavioral, Marxist, anarchist, ecological, and complexity economics sit side by side. Each idea gets a plain-language explanation, a real-world sighting, its common misuse, and threads connecting it to other concepts in the grammar. The goal is not to judge which lens is right, but to make the ideas accessible and see where they weave — or don't.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: William Hogarth's satirical prints on commerce and morality (18th century). Honoré Daumier's lithographs of capitalists and workers (1830s-1870s). Diego Rivera's murals depicting labor and industry. Illustrations from Adam Smith's 'Wealth of Nations' early editions. Soviet constructivist economic posters (1920s-1930s). WPA-era American economic education posters.

egyptian-ideas-future-life.name: Egyptian Ideas of the Future Life
egyptian-ideas-future-life.description:
E.A. Wallis Budge's account of the principal ideas and beliefs held by the ancient Egyptians concerning God, the resurrection, and the future life. Covers the belief in one supreme God (Neter), the myth of Osiris, the nature of the Egyptian pantheon, the Judgment of the Dead in the Hall of Maati, and the doctrines of resurrection, the ka, the ba, and immortality. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #11277 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/11277)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The eight illustrations from the original 1899/1908 edition by Budge, including The Creation, Isis Suckling Horus, the Judgment Scene from the Papyrus of Ani, and the Sekhet-Aaru (Elysian Fields) from the papyri of Nebseni, Ani, and Anilai.

egyptian-kids.name: The Sun's Children: Stories from the Nile
egyptian-kids.description:
Egyptian mythology in rhyming verse for bedtime. The birth of the sun from the primordial waters, Osiris and Isis's love that conquered death, Horus's battle to reclaim the throne, Ra's nightly journey through the underworld, the animal-headed gods who walked among mortals, and the great weighing of the heart that decided your eternity.

This grammar draws from 3,000 years of Egyptian sacred texts — the Pyramid Texts, Coffin Texts, and Book of the Dead — alongside temple inscriptions and papyri. Egyptian mythology was never one fixed story but a living tradition where different cities told different versions. Where stories vary between Heliopolis, Memphis, and Thebes, we follow the most widely known version and note the variations in the Whisper sections.

Source: E.A. Wallis Budge translations (public domain), Project Gutenberg eBook #7145 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/7145).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Egyptian tomb paintings — vibrant flat-color figures in profile with hieroglyphic borders. Papyrus of Ani (Book of the Dead, c. 1250 BCE) — the most beautiful surviving illustrated papyrus, in the British Museum. Descriptions de l'Égypte (1809-1829) — Napoleon's expedition artists, extraordinary temple and monument illustrations. David Roberts (1796-1864) — luminous lithographs of Egyptian temples. Karl Richard Lepsius (1810-1884) — Denkmäler aus Aegypten, precise archaeological illustrations of tomb paintings.

egyptian-kids-storyboard.name: The Sun's Children: Stories from the Nile — Storyboard
egyptian-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on The Sun's Children: Stories from the Nile. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

egyptian-mythology.name: Myths and Legends of Ancient Egypt
egyptian-mythology.description:
Myths and Legends of Ancient Egypt by Lewis Spence (1915), a comprehensive survey of Egyptian mythology, religion, history, magic, literature, and art. Covers the great gods (Osiris, Isis, Ra, Horus, Thoth, Set), the priesthood and temple mysteries, Egyptian literature and magical practices, sacred animal cults, and the artistic tradition. Illustrated by Evelyn Paul.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #43662 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/43662)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Evelyn Paul's original colour plates and line drawings for this 1915 edition — richly detailed depictions of Egyptian gods, temples, and mythological scenes in an Arts and Crafts-influenced style. Also: the tomb paintings and papyrus illustrations reproduced in E.A. Wallis Budge's editions of the Book of the Dead (1895-1913), and the Egyptian archaeological illustrations in Description de l'Egypte (1809-1829).

eleusinian-mysteries.name: The Eleusinian Mysteries and Rites
eleusinian-mysteries.description:
Dudley Wright's study of the most famous mystery cult of the ancient world: the Eleusinian Mysteries sacred to Demeter and Persephone. Covers the founding myth, ritual programme, initiatory rites, and mystical significance of a tradition that endured for nearly two millennia.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #35087 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/35087)

embodied-grammars-constellation.name: Embodied Grammars: A Constellation
embodied-grammars-constellation.description:
A map of maps. This meta grammar connects seven grammars of embodied knowledge — Yoga, Indian Classical Dance, Ballet, Modern Dance, Anthropological Dance, Theater, and Movement Culture. Each item here is a doorway into a full grammar. Together, they trace humanity's relationship to the moving body: as prayer, as protest, as play, as architecture, as liberation. The body is the first instrument; these are its many tunings.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Eadweard Muybridge, *Animal Locomotion* (1887). Auguste Rodin, drawings of Cambodian dancers (1906). Henri Matisse, *La Danse* (1910). Nataraja bronze sculptures (Chola dynasty, 10th-12th century).

emerald-death-card-no-one-here-gets-out-.name: Emerald Death Card: No One Here Gets Out Alive
emerald-death-card-no-one-here-gets-out-.description: A visual and contemplative journey through death traditions, mythology, and transformation. Based on The Emerald podcast episode 'No One Here Gets Out Alive' (Sep 17, 2022), this sequence explores death through Ancient Egyptian funerary texts, Tibetan Bardo practices, Japanese death poems, the Epic of Gilgamesh, and the ecological cycling of matter. Death is not something to flee from, but something to navigate—to practice while alive. As the podcast asks: Will we crumble or will we soar? We will both crumble and soar.

emerald-death-card-visuals.name: Emerald Death Card: No One Here Gets Out Alive
emerald-death-card-visuals.description: A visual and contemplative journey through death traditions, mythology, and transformation. Based on The Emerald podcast episode 'No One Here Gets Out Alive' (Sep 17, 2022), this sequence explores death through Ancient Egyptian funerary texts, Tibetan Bardo practices, Japanese death poems, the Epic of Gilgamesh, and the ecological cycling of matter. Death is not something to flee from, but something to navigate—to practice while alive. As the podcast asks: Will we crumble or will we soar? We will both crumble and soar.

emerald-kids-folk-tales.name: Emerald Kids: Folk Tales & Mythology for Young Listeners
emerald-kids-folk-tales.description: A kid-friendly playlist of animated folk tales and mythology videos inspired by stories referenced in The Emerald podcast. This playlist starts with joyful creation stories, Mother Earth celebrations, and nature myths before exploring tricksters, transformations, and life's deeper mysteries. Josh Schrei's podcast explores myth and story through an adult lens, but many of the tales he references have wonderful children's versions. Suitable for young viewers (ages 4-12).

emma-austen.name: Emma
emma-austen.description:
Jane Austen's Emma (1815) — the supreme comedy of self-deception in English literature. Emma Woodhouse, handsome, clever, and rich, sets out to arrange the romantic lives of her neighbors in the village of Highbury, blind to the real movements of every heart around her — especially her own. Fifty-five chapters across three volumes trace her journey from confident matchmaker to humbled lover, as Austen constructs one of the most intricate plots in fiction. Every rereading reveals new layers of irony, foreshadowing, and psychological precision.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #158 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/158)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Hugh Thomson's pen-and-ink illustrations for the 1896 Macmillan edition of Emma — elegant, witty line drawings that perfectly capture Austen's social comedy. C. E. Brock's illustrations for the 1898 Dent edition. Chris Hammond's illustrations for the 1898 George Allen edition.

enchiridion.name: The Enchiridion
enchiridion.description:
The Enchiridion ('Handbook') of Epictetus, a concise manual of Stoic ethics compiled by his student Arrian. 51 maxims on what is in our power vs. what is not — the foundation of Stoic practice that directly influenced CBT, DBT, and modern resilience psychology. Thomas Wentworth Higginson translation.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #45109 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/45109)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Roman marble busts and sculpture. The Farnese Hercules, Capitoline Marcus Aurelius. Neoclassical engravings from 18th-century Epictetus editions.

essays-emerson.name: Essays: First Series
essays-emerson.description:
Ralph Waldo Emerson's Essays: First Series (1841) -- the founding text of American Transcendentalism. Twelve essays that redefined the relationship between the individual and the universe. From the famous Self-Reliance ('Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string') to the mystical Over-Soul ('Within man is the soul of the whole') to the restless Circles ('every action admits of being outdone'), Emerson argues that each person has direct access to the divine mind and owes allegiance to no authority but their own deepest intuition. These essays shaped Thoreau, Whitman, Nietzsche, William James, and the entire tradition of American individualism.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2944 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2944)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs and daguerreotypes of Emerson by various artists. Illustrations of the Concord landscape. Pre-Raphaelite art (Emerson's aesthetic contemporaries).

essentials-of-mysticism.name: The Essentials of Mysticism
essentials-of-mysticism.description:
The Essentials of Mysticism and Other Essays by Evelyn Underhill (1920). Thirteen essays on the theory and practice of mysticism, from its essential nature to its expression in the lives of mystics from Plotinus to Charles Péguy. Underhill — the premier English-language scholar of mysticism in the early twentieth century — offers both rigorous analysis and sympathetic portraiture.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #74203 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/74203)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Pre-Raphaelite and Arts and Crafts movement imagery complements Underhill's sensibility. Also: medieval manuscript illuminations, particularly those depicting contemplative figures and visionary experiences. The photographs and engravings of the mystics discussed (Julian's cell at Norwich, Assisi, Lisieux) would provide visual context.

ethics-spinoza.name: Ethics
ethics-spinoza.description:
Baruch Spinoza's Ethics (Ethica Ordine Geometrico Demonstrata, 1677) — one of the most radical and rigorous works in the history of philosophy. Written in geometric form (definitions, axioms, propositions, proofs), it builds a complete system from the concept of substance through mind-body parallelism, a natural science of the emotions, and an analysis of human bondage, culminating in a vision of intellectual freedom and blessedness. Spinoza's God is not a personal creator but Nature itself: 'Deus sive Natura.' Translated by R. H. M. Elwes (1883).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #3800 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/3800)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Portraits of Spinoza, including the famous painting attributed to Baruch Spinoza (c. 1665). Engravings from early editions of the Opera Posthuma (1677). Images of Spinoza's lens-grinding tools and the Spinoza House in The Hague.

evolution-tree.name: Evolution Tree
evolution-tree.description:
The tree of life — 30 major transitions in evolution, from the first self-replicating molecules to the human mind. Each branch point represents a moment when life invented something new: a new body plan, a new way to eat, a new way to move, a new way to think. Tiered content for young children (Story) and older readers (Science).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Ernst Haeckel (1834–1919), whose 'Kunstformen der Natur' (Art Forms in Nature, 1904) contains spectacular illustrations of radiolarians, jellyfish, and other organisms that celebrate the beauty of evolutionary diversity. Charles Darwin's own tree-of-life sketch from his notebook (1837).

expression-of-emotions.name: The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals
expression-of-emotions.description:
Darwin's groundbreaking study of emotional expression across humans and animals (1872). Combining photography, cross-cultural surveys, infant observation, and comparative anatomy, Darwin argues that our emotions are not cultural inventions but biological inheritances shared with other species. Fourteen chapters trace the expressions of grief, joy, anger, love, fear, shame, and more, establishing the foundations of modern affective science.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1227 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1227)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The book's own photographic plates by Oscar Rejlander and others (1872 edition); drawings by Mr. T.W. Wood and Mr. A. May of animal expressions; the Duchenne de Boulogne photographs of facial muscles and expression (1862).

fairy-tales-south-africa.name: Fairy Tales from South Africa
fairy-tales-south-africa.description:
Eighteen fairy tales collected from the Swazi, Zulu, Shangani, and Msuto peoples of South Africa by Mrs. E. J. Bourhill and Mrs. J. B. Drake (1908). These traditional tales, told around evening fires by grandmothers and master storytellers, feature enchanted princes in animal form, shining princesses, fairy birds, serpent bridegrooms, and the ever-present theme of transformation. They share deep structural roots with European fairy tales (Beauty and the Beast, Cinderella, The Frog Prince) while remaining distinctly African in setting, spirit, and moral sensibility. The stories come from the mountain valleys and wooded lowlands of Swaziland, Natal, and the Eastern Transvaal. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #75833 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/75833).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: W. Herbert Holloway's original illustrations for the 1908 Macmillan and Co. edition — evocative pen-and-ink drawings depicting Setuli and the Fairy, the mountains of Swaziland, warriors in leopard skins, and the fantastical creatures of South African folklore.

five-stages-greek-religion.name: Five Stages of Greek Religion
five-stages-greek-religion.description:
Gilbert Murray's classic study of the evolution of Greek religious thought, from primitive fertility rites through Olympian theology, classical philosophy, Hellenistic mysticism, and the final pagan resistance to Christianity. A foundational text in the history of religion.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #30250 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/30250)

folk-stories-southern-nigeria.name: Folk Stories from Southern Nigeria
folk-stories-southern-nigeria.description:
Forty folk stories from the Cross River region of Southern Nigeria, collected by Elphinstone Dayrell, District Commissioner, with an introduction by Andrew Lang (1910). These tales from the Efik and Ibibio peoples feature the cunning tortoise as trickster hero, powerful Ju Ju magic, the fearsome Egbo secret societies, and a rich bestiary of animal characters whose conflicts explain the origins of the natural world. The stories blend earthy humor with moral instruction, supernatural terror with social commentary, and reveal a complex civilization where beauty is dangerous, wisdom is survival, and every creature has its story. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #34655 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/34655).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Major G. M. de L. Dayrell's color drawing of a Ju-Ju mask from Ibo Country (frontispiece of the 1910 Longmans, Green and Co. edition). The original 1910 edition contains no other illustrations but the mask drawing is a striking representation of the spiritual world these tales inhabit.

folk-tales-tibet.name: Folk Tales from Tibet
folk-tales-tibet.description:
Twenty-two folk tales collected and translated by Captain W. F. O'Connor during the British Mission to Lhasa (1904). Animal trickster tales, magical adventures, and moral fables from the oral tradition of Tibet.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #75000 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/75000)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Original illustrations by an unnamed Tibetan artist resident at Gyantse, commissioned for the 1906 Hurst and Blackett edition. Traditional Tibetan folk art style.

folklore-north-american-indian.name: Folk-Lore and Legends: North American Indian
folklore-north-american-indian.description:
Thirty-one folk tales from North American Indian traditions, collected and published anonymously in London in 1890. The collection features the great trickster Manabozho, journeys to the spirit world, tales of transformation and shape-shifting, and stories of love between humans and the living natural world. These tales illuminate an animist cosmology in which trees speak, the dead are near, and the boundary between human and animal is fluid and permeable. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #22072 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/22072).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: No specific illustrator credited in the 1890 W. W. Gibbings edition. George Catlin's paintings of Native American life (1830s-1840s) and Karl Bodmer's illustrations from 'Travels in the Interior of North America' (1832-1834) provide excellent period visual companions for these tales.

frederick-douglass.name: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
frederick-douglass.description:
Frederick Douglass's 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave, Written by Himself' (1845) — one of the most powerful and influential autobiographies in American literature. Born into slavery in Maryland, Douglass taught himself to read, fought his overseer, escaped to freedom, and became the most prominent voice of the abolitionist movement. This Narrative is testimony, argument, and literature at once: a first-person account that dismantled every justification for slavery and changed the conscience of a nation.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #23 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Daguerreotypes and photographs of Frederick Douglass (the most photographed American of the 19th century). Illustrations from the 1845 first edition published by the Anti-Slavery Office, Boston. Woodcuts and engravings from abolitionist publications including The Liberator. Portraits by Elisha Hammond and other period artists.

gestures-towards-balanced-future.name: Gestures Towards a More Balanced Future
gestures-towards-balanced-future.description:
A homage to Vanessa Andreotti's work, structured as a single tree — the Tree of Coloniality — with its roots in separation, its trunk in addiction, its canopy blocking light, its fruits rotting, and its seeds trying to sprout. The grammar does not offer solutions. It offers gestures. At every scale — from soil to cosmos — it names what it cannot hold, confessing its own incompleteness. The end of the Anthropocene is a very small chapter in the cosmological story. Spirit pervades every level. Nobody knows what comes next.

Cross-references grammars across the recursive.eco library: Decolonization Five Domains, I Ching, Jungian Archetypes, Inner Triangle, Yoga Body as Temple, Tao Te Ching, Alice in Wonderland, Aesop's Fables, and others.

giras-do-sitio.name: As Giras do Sítio — When Vovó Nastácia Came Home
giras-do-sitio.description:
A children's grammar where Tia Nastácia returns as a preta velha in Umbanda to speak with Pedro (Pedrinho grown up) and his family. Each story is a gira — a spiritual gathering where ancestors come to teach, heal, and compost the colonial stories we inherited. Emília, the rag doll Nastácia once sewed, becomes the bridge between the children and the spirit world: the erê, the sacred child energy that makes the invisible visible. The family is reading Sítio do Picapau Amarelo together and writing a new story in the margins — one where the cook was always the author, the doll was always alive, and the forest was always listening.

Age tiers: 'For Little Ones' (5+) uses simple sensory language and gentle prescriptions. 'For Growing Ones' (8+) introduces more complex themes. 'For the Whole Family' touches decolonial questions that adults and children can sit with together.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura) — the folk art tradition of northeastern Brazil, perfect for depicting terreiro scenes and spiritual entities. J. Borges' cordel woodcuts (public domain reproductions). Illustrations from Percy Lau's 'Tipos e Aspectos do Brasil' (1940s IBGE series) depicting Brazilian folk life. Afro-Brazilian religious art from Pierre Verger's photographs of Candomblé/Umbanda ceremonies (selected public domain images, 1940s-50s).

gitanjali.name: Gitanjali: Song Offerings
gitanjali.description:
Gitanjali ('Song Offerings'), 103 prose poems by Rabindranath Tagore, translated from Bengali by the author himself. These devotional poems won the 1913 Nobel Prize in Literature — the first non-European to receive it. W.B.
