Yeats wrote in his introduction: 'I have carried the manuscript of these translations about with me for days, reading it in railway trains, or on the top of omnibuses and in restaurants, and I have often had to close it lest some stranger would see how much it moved me.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #7164 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/7164)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Abanindranath Tagore's Bengal School paintings, Nandalal Bose's ink wash illustrations. The gentle watercolor style of early 20th century Bengali art — lotus flowers, rivers, figures in devotion.

gnosis-of-light.name: The Gnosis of the Light
gnosis-of-light.description:
Translation and commentary on the Codex Brucianus, an ancient Gnostic text describing the emanation of divine light and the structure of the spiritual universe. Rev. F. Lamplugh's 1918 English rendering of the Untitled Apocalypse, a second-century work from the Codex Brucianus (Bodleian Library, Oxford), with introduction and explanatory notes.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #30799 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/30799)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The Codex Brucianus Gnostic Cross frontispiece (original MS, Bodleian Library); William Blake's visionary illustrations of cosmic emanation (1790s-1820s); Athanasius Kircher's diagrams of cosmic hierarchies from Oedipus Aegyptiacus (1652-1654).

gnostic-crucifixion.name: The Gnostic Crucifixion
gnostic-crucifixion.description:
G.R.S. Mead's study of the Gnostic interpretation of the crucifixion, exploring how early Gnostic Christians understood the cross as a cosmic symbol rather than a historical event. Based on the newly-found fragments of The Acts of John, this 1907 work presents the Vision of the Cross with Mead's detailed commentary drawing on Valentinian, Naassene, Docetic, and Hermetic sources.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #35735 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/35735)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Early Christian cross symbols from the Roman catacombs (2nd-4th century); the Chi-Rho and Christogram from Constantine's era; Coptic cross designs from Egyptian manuscripts; William Blake's crucifixion and resurrection illustrations.

golden-ass-apuleius.name: The Golden Ass (Metamorphoses)
golden-ass-apuleius.description:
The only complete Latin novel surviving from antiquity — Apuleius's tale of Lucius, transformed into a donkey by misadventure with magic, who endures every form of human cruelty and comedy before being saved by the goddess Isis. At its center: the tale of Cupid and Psyche, the original Beauty and the Beast, Jung's favorite myth of the soul's journey to love. William Adlington's Elizabethan translation (1566).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1666 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1666).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: William Morris and Edward Burne-Jones illustrations for the Kelmscott Press Cupid and Psyche (1897). Raphael's Loggia paintings of Cupid and Psyche (1518). Antonio Canova's sculptures of Psyche. Classical Roman frescoes from Pompeii.

golden-bough.name: The Golden Bough
golden-bough.description:
Sir James George Frazer's The Golden Bough: A Study of Magic and Religion (Abridged Edition, 1922) — the foundational work of comparative mythology. In 69 chapters, Frazer traces the institution of sacred kingship from the grove of Diana at Nemi through the world's myths, rituals, and folk customs: sympathetic magic, tree worship, the dying gods Adonis, Attis, Osiris, and Dionysus, corn-spirits, scapegoats, fire festivals, and the external soul. A vast cabinet of wonders that shaped modern understanding of myth and ritual.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #3623 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/3623)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: J.M.W. Turner's painting 'The Golden Bough' (1834, Tate Gallery). Illustrations from the 12-volume third edition (Macmillan, 1906-1915). Ancient Roman artifacts depicting Diana of Nemi. Victorian engravings of European harvest customs and fire festivals.

gospel-of-buddha.name: The Gospel of Buddha
gospel-of-buddha.description:
The Gospel of Buddha, compiled from ancient records by Paul Carus (1894, illustrated edition 1915). A harmonized retelling of the life and teachings of Siddhattha Gotama drawn from Pali, Sanskrit, and Chinese sources. One hundred chapters trace the arc from the Bodhisatta's birth through enlightenment, the founding of the Sangha, decades of teaching through sermons and parables, to the final entering into Nirvana at Kusinara. Carus sought to present Buddhism's essential spirit on common ground shared by all schools.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #35895 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/35895)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Olga Kopetzky (1915, Open Court Publishing), pen-and-ink drawings inspired by Ajanta cave paintings and Gandhara sculpture. Classical Buddhist art style with European draftsmanship. Also consider: illustrations from Edwin Arnold's 'The Light of Asia' (various editions, 1879+).

greek-mythology.name: Greek Mythology & Astrology
greek-mythology.description: The gods and heroes of Greek mythology as a living family tree and astrology source. Planet-gods use English names for chart compatibility. Zodiac signs are the mythological characters placed in the sky. Myths weave gods, heroes, and signs together through composite_of connections, drawing a constellation map of the ancient world.

grimms-fairy-tales.name: Grimm's Fairy Tales
grimms-fairy-tales.description:
The fairy tales collected by Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm, first published as 'Kinder- und Hausmärchen' (Children's and Household Tales) in 1812. These 62 tales have shaped the Western imagination for over 200 years — but they are not neutral folklore. The Grimms collected primarily from literate middle-class families (not peasants), rewrote tales across seven editions to add Christian morality and Germanic nationalism, and the collection was later weaponized by Nazi propaganda as proof of an eternal 'German spirit.' The tales were further laundered through Disney into 'universal' children's culture, erasing both their editorial history and the (mostly female) storytellers. The stories themselves remain beautiful and worth reading — but honest framing matters. Margaret Hunt translation (1884). Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2591 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2591).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's 1909 illustrations for 'The Fairy Tales of the Brothers Grimm' (Constable) — atmospheric pen-and-ink and watercolor illustrations, public domain. Walter Crane's 1882 'Household Stories from the Brothers Grimm' — Art Nouveau-influenced woodcuts, public domain. George Cruikshank's 1823 illustrations for the first English edition — early Victorian etchings, public domain. Ludwig Emil Grimm's (Wilhelm's brother) original illustrations from early German editions. Philipp Grot Johann and Robert Leinweber's 1893 illustrations — detailed pen-and-ink, public domain. Kay Nielsen's 1925 illustrations for select tales — Art Deco fairy tale art, public domain in most jurisdictions. Gustave Doré's illustrations for 'Perrault's Fairy Tales' (1862) — overlap with several Grimm tales (Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Red Riding Hood).

hawaiian-legends.name: Legends of Old Honolulu
hawaiian-legends.description:
Legends of Old Honolulu (Mythology), collected and translated from the Hawaiian by W.D. Westervelt (1916). Twenty-five legends of gods, shapeshifters, ghost dancers, and heroes rooted in the places of Honolulu and Oahu — the Polynesian migration, the sacred Water of Life, Kamapuaa the hog-god, the ghost dance on Punchbowl, the owls of Honolulu, and more. A window into the Hawaiian mythological imagination where landscape and story are inseparable.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #66547 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/66547)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The original 1916 Ellis Press edition includes photographs of Honolulu landscapes. Charles W. Bartlett's Hawaiian color woodblock prints (1910s-1920s). D. Howard Hitchcock's landscape paintings of Hawaii (early 1900s). Arman Manookian's Hawaiian art (1920s).

hero-myths-british.name: Hero-Myths & Legends of the British Race
hero-myths-british.description:
Sixteen hero-legends from across the British Isles, retold by M. I. Ebbutt (1910). From the Anglo-Saxon Beowulf to Celtic Cuchulain to Robin Hood, these tales trace the evolution of British heroic ideals across a thousand years.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #25502 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/25502)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: J. H. F. Bacon A.R.A., Byam Shaw, W. H. Margetson R.I., Gertrude Demain Hammond — original illustrations from the 1910 George G. Harrap & Company edition.

hesiod-homeric-hymns.name: Hesiod, the Homeric Hymns, and Homerica
hesiod-homeric-hymns.description:
The oldest surviving Greek sacred poetry: Hesiod's Theogony (birth of the gods from Chaos), Works and Days (the farmer's ethics and the five ages of man), the Shield of Heracles, and thirty-three Homeric Hymns — invocations of the Olympian gods from Demeter to Dionysus. These are the foundation texts of Greek mythology: the primary sources for Prometheus, Persephone, Pandora, and the entire Olympian pantheon.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #348 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/348). Hugh G. Evelyn-White translation (1914).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John Flaxman's line illustrations for Hesiod (1817). Greek vase paintings of the Olympian gods. Walter Crane's illustrations of Greek myths.

hidden-symbolism-alchemy.name: Hidden Symbolism of Alchemy and the Occult Arts
hidden-symbolism-alchemy.description:
Herbert Silberer's groundbreaking 1914 study connecting alchemical symbolism to psychoanalysis — the missing link between Freud and Jung. Through analysis of an alchemical parable, Silberer explores alchemy, Hermeticism, Rosicrucianism, and the psychology of spiritual transformation.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #27755 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/27755).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Alchemical woodcuts from the Rosarium Philosophorum (1550). Emblems from Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens (1617). Illustrations from Basil Valentine's The Twelve Keys.

history-of-magic-levi.name: The History of Magic
history-of-magic-levi.description:
The History of Magic by Eliphas Levi (1860), translated by Arthur Edward Waite (1922) — a comprehensive survey of magic from its mythological origins through ancient civilizations, medieval alchemy and sorcery, to the occultism of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. Levi presents magic as the science of the ancient magi, tracing its thread through the Kabalah, Hermeticism, and the Western esoteric tradition.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #70033 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/70033)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The original 1922 Rider edition includes numerous illustrations. Additional relevant imagery: the Baphomet drawing by Eliphas Levi, engravings from Levi's Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie (1856), Kabalistic diagrams from the Sepher Yetzirah tradition, and portraits of Levi from the 19th century.

homer-kids.name: The Wine-Dark Sea: Homer for Bedtime
homer-kids.description:
The Iliad and Odyssey retold as cosmic mythology for bedtime. 42 stories in rhyming verse with an esoteric, animist lens — the gods as vast cosmic intelligences, Odysseus as shaman-trickster, the Trojan War as the moment humanity stepped out of the gods' shadow. Designed for 5-minute bedtime reading with karaoke-style TTS and full-bleed illustrations.

Source: Original retellings from memory, drawing on Samuel Butler's prose translations of the Iliad (Project Gutenberg eBook #2199, https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2199) and Odyssey (Project Gutenberg eBook #1727, https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1727).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John Flaxman's neoclassical line drawings for the Iliad and Odyssey (1793) — ~75 pure line illustrations, elegant and iconic. Greek red-figure and black-figure vase paintings (6th-4th century BCE) from the Metropolitan Museum Open Access collection — bold silhouettes and narrative scenes. William Russell Flint's Odyssey watercolors (1924) — lush color plates for variety.

homer-kids-storyboard.name: The Wine-Dark Sea: Homer for Bedtime — Storyboard
homer-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on The Wine-Dark Sea: Homer for Bedtime. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

human-body.name: The Human Body: An Emergent Symphony
human-body.description:
An exploration of the human body as an interconnected symphony — from individual cells to whole-body emergence. Covers 12 body systems with ~65 components, each described through function, connection, wonder, and vulnerability. Built for curious minds of all ages.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Henry Gray, *Anatomy of the Human Body* (1918 edition, illustrations by Henry Vandyke Carter), Bartleby.com. Leonardo da Vinci anatomical drawings (Royal Collection). Andreas Vesalius, *De Humani Corporis Fabrica* (1543), woodcut illustrations.

i-ching-chinese-emergent.name: 易經 · Emergent Structure (Lines → Trigrams → Hexagrams)
i-ching-chinese-emergent.description:
The I Ching reorganized to mirror its own cosmological emergence: from the two primordial lines (陰 yin and 陽 yang), through the eight trigrams (八卦 bāguà), to the sixty-four hexagrams (六十四卦). Classical Chinese text throughout, with brief English translations for lines and trigrams. Hexagram text extracted from Project Gutenberg eBook #25501. Trigram descriptions from the 說卦傳 (Shuo Gua Zhuan / Discussion of Trigrams). Line philosophy from the 繫辭傳 (Xi Ci Zhuan / Great Commentary).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #25501 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/25501)

i-ching-chinese-original-with-brief-tran.name: I Ching - Chinese Original With Brief Translation.
i-ching-chinese-original-with-brief-tran.description: This was extracted from Project Gutenberg's Archives. https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/25501. AI made short translations to the text (Claude Code)

i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.name: I Ching HD Meta-Categories: Three Lenses for Viewing the 64 Hexagrams (My Version) (Copy)
i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.description: This interpretation maps the 64 I Ching hexagrams to Human Design 'gates'—a synthesis channeled by Ra Uru Hu in 1987 after an eight-day encounter with 'the Voice' on Ibiza. The system is only about 30 years old, which means we're still discovering what it actually describes, if anything beyond symbolic vocabulary. Ra claimed the Voice transmitted a complete system mapping I Ching hexagrams around an astrological wheel in specific relationships to planetary positions. Whether this represents genuine cosmic blueprints, a playful reconfiguration of ancient symbolic systems, or simply altered consciousness creating coherent pattern, remains genuinely open. What's documented: many people find the 'types' (Manifestor, Generator, Projector, Reflector, Manifesting Generator) and gate activations offer useful language for recognizing their patterns. The identity game is always dangerous, especially when systems are lifted from their traditional contexts and sold as objective truth. Over-identification with any typology can become a cage. That said, personally discovering I was supposedly a 'Projector' rather than trying to operate like a 'Generator' released me from exhausting patterns—it gave me permission to develop recursive.eco intimately rather than forcing myself into productivity modes that never fit. That release alone makes the system worth studying, even if it's 'just' a useful fiction. Whether it serves you is yours to discover. The gates maintain exact I Ching numbering (Gate 1 = Hexagram 1, etc.) and are arranged around the Rave Mandala wheel in Fu Xi sequence. Each gate occupies approximately 5.625 degrees, with some gates spanning two zodiac signs. Use this as contemplative material for self-reflection and pattern recognition, not as fixed identity or limitation. Hold it lightly. Fork it freely.

i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.name: I Ching HD Meta-Categories: Three Lenses for Viewing the 64 Hexagrams (My Version) (Copy)
i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.description: This interpretation maps the 64 I Ching hexagrams to Human Design 'gates'—a synthesis channeled by Ra Uru Hu in 1987 after an eight-day encounter with 'the Voice' on Ibiza. i-ching-leibniz-binary.name: 易經 · Leibniz Binary Tree of Change (Lines → Trigrams → Hexagrams)
i-ching-leibniz-binary.description:
The I Ching mapped as a 6-dimensional binary hypercube, following the insight Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz had in 1703 when he recognized the Fu Xi/Shao Yong hexagram sequence as binary counting from 0 (坤 Kun, all yin) to 63 (乾 Qian, all yang). 384 individual lines serve as transformation edges — when a line changes, it flips one bit and transforms one hexagram into another. The 64 hexagrams are vertices of the hypercube, connected by their 6 lines to 6 neighboring hexagrams. 8 trigrams represent the 3-bit building blocks. Each hexagram carries 7 historical relationship systems in its metadata: 綜卦 (inverse), 錯卦 (complement), 互卦 (nuclear), 序卦 (King Wen pair), 京房八宮 (Jing Fang's Eight Palaces), 馬王堆 (Mawangdui sequence), and 雜卦傳 (Miscellaneous Notes pairings). All hexagram text is classical Chinese from Project Gutenberg eBook #25501.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #25501 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/25501)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The Leibniz-Bouvet diagram (1703) showing the 64 hexagrams arranged in binary order, housed at the Niedersächsische Landesbibliothek, Hanover. Shao Yong's Xiantian circular arrangement from various Song dynasty editions.

imitation-of-christ.name: The Imitation of Christ
imitation-of-christ.description:
The Imitation of Christ by Thomas à Kempis (c. 1418-1427) is the most widely read Christian devotional work after the Bible. Four books of practical mysticism: from external discipline through interior silence to mystical dialogue with Christ and sacramental union. William Benham translation.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1653 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1653)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Medieval illuminated manuscript traditions, particularly the Devotio Moderna style of the Low Countries (15th century). The woodcuts of early printed editions (1470s-1500s) capture the austere beauty of the text. Also: Fra Angelico's frescoes at San Marco, Florence — their luminous simplicity matches Thomas's devotional intensity.

indian-classical-dance.name: Indian Classical Dance: Natya — The Fifth Veda
indian-classical-dance.description:
The Natyashastra, attributed to Bharata Muni (~200 BCE), is sometimes called the Fifth Veda — a scripture of embodied knowledge where every gesture is a word, every expression a sentence, every dance a prayer. But the text's deepest meaning was unlocked by Abhinavagupta (c. 950-1020 CE), the great Tantric master of Kashmir, who wrote both the Tantraloka (the encyclopedia of non-dual Shaiva Tantra) and the Abhinavabharati (the definitive commentary on the Natyashastra). Abhinavagupta saw rasa — the aesthetic experience of dance and drama — as identical to spiritual liberation. The body in motion is not preparing for transcendence; it IS transcendence. This grammar maps the nine rasas, the mudra vocabulary, the eight classical forms, and the repertoire structure through this Tantric lens: the dancing body as the site of awakening.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Illustrations from early translations of the Natyashastra. Bronze Chola dynasty Nataraja sculptures (10th-12th century). Tanjore paintings of dancers (18th-19th century). Ajanta cave murals depicting dance postures (2nd century BCE - 6th century CE). Kashmir miniature paintings depicting court scenes and devotional imagery.

indian-fairy-tales.name: Indian Fairy Tales
indian-fairy-tales.description:
Twenty-nine fairy tales from the Indian subcontinent, selected and edited by Joseph Jacobs (1892) with illustrations by John D. Batten. Drawing from the ancient Jatakas (Buddhist birth stories), Sanskrit collections, and modern Indian folk-tale collectors, this landmark anthology gathers animal fables, quest tales, stories of wit and magic, and legends of love and wisdom from one of the richest storytelling traditions on Earth. Jacobs called it 'a sort of Indian Grimm' — these are the tales from which much of the world's fairy tale heritage ultimately springs. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #7128 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/7128).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John D. Batten's original illustrations for the 1892 David Nutt edition — beautiful line drawings and color plates in Arts and Crafts style depicting scenes from each tale. Warwick Goble's illustrations for 'Indian Myth and Legend' by Donald A. Mackenzie (1913, Gresham Publishing) — watercolor plates of Indian scenes. W. Heath Robinson's illustrations for 'A Song of the English' and Indian subjects (early 1900s).

indian-myth-legend.name: Indian Myth and Legend
indian-myth-legend.description:
Donald A. Mackenzie's comprehensive retelling of Indian mythology (1913) — from the Vedic gods (Indra, Agni, Varuna, Yama) through the great epics: the complete Mahābhārata (including the Bhagavad Gita) and the Rāmāyaṇa. 26 chapters covering creation myths, cosmic cycles, the philosophy of dharma, and the most beloved stories of Hindu civilization.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #47228 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/47228).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Warwick Goble's watercolour illustrations from the original 1913 edition (ethereal, Pre-Raphaelite style). Raja Ravi Varma's paintings of Hindu deities and epic scenes (1890s). Mughal miniature paintings of Rama, Krishna, and the Pandavas.

inner-triangle.name: The Inner Triangle — Emotion, Cognition & Behavior in Relation
inner-triangle.description:
A 68-card oracle deck mapping the CBT triangle — Feel, Think, Do — placed in the relational field. Every emotion asks: what does your partner see? Every thought asks: what would their version be? Every behavior asks: what does it trigger across from you? 3 suits (Emotions, Cognitions, Behaviors), 14 L2 named triangles mapping specific loops (The Anxious Triangle, The Avoidant Triangle, The Shame-Hide Triangle, The Vulnerability-Connection Triangle), and 4 L3 meta-patterns. Companion grammar to Relationship Cards.

Grounded in CBT (Beck), DBT (Linehan), EFT (Johnson), attachment theory (Bowlby), affect theory (Tomkins), schema therapy (Young), polyvagal theory (Porges), and mentalization (Fonagy).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Edvard Munch's expressionist paintings of emotion — 'The Scream' (1893), 'Anxiety' (1894), 'Melancholy' (1894-96), 'Jealousy' (1895). Käthe Kollwitz's charcoal drawings of grief, tenderness, and maternal emotion (1900s-1930s). Francisco Goya's 'Black Paintings' (1819-1823) — Saturn, the dog, the witches' sabbath. William Blake's illustrations of states of being from 'Songs of Innocence and Experience' (1789-1794). Honoré Daumier's lithographs of human expression and social emotion (1830s-1870s). Charles Le Brun's 'Conference on Expression' (1668) — systematic drawings of facial expressions mapping emotion to physiology.

inspiration.name: Inspiration: Podcasts, Books & Music
inspiration.description:
A personal library of inspirations — podcasts that shaped my thinking, books that opened doors, and music that holds the soul. Each item carries my own reflection on why it matters. This grammar is a living recommendation list, a map of the voices and works that feed recursive.eco.

Migrated from PlayfulProcess Ghost website (2025).

instituto-canoa-math-6.name: Curupira's Math Forest — Grade 6
instituto-canoa-math-6.description:
Twenty-five group math activities for 6th graders, guided by the Curupira — a forest guardian spirit from Tupi-Guarani mythology with flame-red hair and backwards feet. Based on Instituto Canoa's collaborative math activities designed for Brazilian public schools.

The Curupira doesn't teach math — the Curupira shows children that math already lives in the forest: in the angles of branches, the symmetry of leaves, the probability of rain, and the fractions of shared fruit. Each activity is hands-on, group-based, and designed so that every student contributes.

Instituto Canoa (institutocanoa.org) is a Brazilian non-profit supporting high-quality math education in public schools, in partnership with the Lemann Center at Stanford University.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura) — the traditional northeastern Brazilian folk art of hand-carved woodblock prints. The Curupira is a public domain figure from indigenous Tupi-Guarani mythology, first documented by José de Anchieta in 1560.

interpretation-of-dreams.name: The Interpretation of Dreams
interpretation-of-dreams.description:
Sigmund Freud's foundational text of psychoanalysis (1900), in which he argues that dreams are the disguised fulfilment of repressed wishes and develops the method of free association for their interpretation. This grammar presents all seven chapters plus introductory material, covering the scientific literature on dreams, the dream of Irma's injection, wish-fulfilment theory, dream distortion, sources of dream material, the dream-work (condensation, displacement, symbolism, secondary elaboration), and the psychology of the dream activities. Translated by A. A. Brill (1913).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #66048 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/66048)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The original German editions featured no illustrations, but later editions and related works offer visual material. Consider Max Halberstadt's iconic 1921 photograph of Freud at his desk; the surrealist dream imagery of Salvador Dalí (who met Freud in 1938); and Art Nouveau book designs of the early Vienna Secession period (Koloman Moser, Gustav Klimt) which were contemporary with the book's first publication.

japanese-fairy-tales.name: Japanese Fairy Tales
japanese-fairy-tales.description:
Twenty-two classic Japanese fairy tales compiled by Yei Theodora Ozaki (1908), translated from the modern Japanese versions of Sadanami Sanjin. These beloved stories — including Momotaro, Urashima Taro, Kaguya-hime, and Kintaro — are the foundational fairy tales of Japan, featuring brave warriors, clever animals, enchanted princesses, and journeys to supernatural realms. They carry deep moral teachings about courage, kindness, loyalty, and the consequences of greed. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #4018 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/4018).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Kakuzo Fujiyama's original illustrations for the 1908 Archibald Constable & Co. edition of 'Japanese Fairy Tales' — color plates in traditional Japanese art style. Hasegawa Takejiro's 'Japanese Fairy Tale Series' (1885-1922, T. Hasegawa, Tokyo) — beautiful color woodblock-printed crêpe paper books, public domain. Warwick Goble's illustrations for 'Green Willow and Other Japanese Fairy Tales' (1910, Macmillan) — watercolor illustrations of Japanese scenes and characters, public domain.

jungian-archetypes.name: Jungian Archetypes — The Inner Cast
jungian-archetypes.description:
A 34-card oracle deck based on Carl Jung's theory of archetypes and the collective unconscious. 24 L1 archetypes (Ego, Shadow, Anima, Animus, Self, Persona, Great Mother, Wise Old Man, Trickster, Divine Child, Hero, and more), 6 L2 archetypal dynamics (Shadow Meets Ego, Persona-Shadow Split, Hero and the Dragon), and 4 L3 individuation stages (Individuation, Night Sea Journey, Sacred Marriage, The Return). Built from memory, drawing on Jung's Collected Works, James Hillman's archetypal psychology, Marie-Louise von Franz's fairy tale analyses, and Joseph Campbell's hero's journey.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Images from Jung's 'Red Book' (Liber Novus) — Jung's own paintings and calligraphy of archetypal visions (published 2009, images from 1914-1930). Alchemical illustrations from 'Rosarium Philosophorum' (1550) — the alchemical marriage sequence Jung used extensively, public domain. Illustrations from 'Atalanta Fugiens' by Michael Maier (1617) — alchemical emblems, public domain. Tarot de Marseille imagery (17th-18th century) — the original tarot archetypes that influenced Jung's thinking. William Blake's mythological paintings and engravings (1790s-1820s) — archetypal imagery of creation, fall, and redemption. Gustave Doré's illustrations of Dante's 'Divine Comedy' (1861-1868) — descent, transformation, and return imagery. Ancient Greek vase paintings depicting mythological archetypes (Hero, Trickster, Great Mother). Hindu and Buddhist mandala imagery — Jung drew heavily on mandala symbolism for the Self archetype.

jungle-book.name: The Jungle Book
jungle-book.description:
Rudyard Kipling's masterwork of animal fable and adventure (1894) -- seven stories and seven companion poems set in the jungles of India, the seal beaches of the Bering Sea, and the military camps of the British Raj. At its heart are the Mowgli stories: a human child raised by wolves who must find his identity between the jungle and the village. Alongside them, Rikki-tikki-tavi battles cobras in a garden, Kotick the white seal searches the oceans for sanctuary, Little Toomai witnesses the secret elephant dance, and the camp animals debate duty and fear. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #35997 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/35997).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The original 1894 Century Co. edition illustrated by John Lockwood Kipling (Rudyard's father), W.H. Drake, and P. Frenzeny -- detailed pen-and-ink drawings of jungle animals, Indian landscapes, and colonial settings. Maurice and Edward Detmold's 1908 illustrations for 'The Jungle Book' (Macmillan) -- lush, painterly watercolors of Mowgli, the wolves, and the jungle, widely considered the finest Jungle Book illustrations. Aldren Watson's 1948 illustrations (Grosset & Dunlap) -- classic mid-century pen-and-ink. Fritz Eichenberg's 1950 woodcut illustrations -- bold, dramatic black-and-white.

just-so-stories.name: Just So Stories
just-so-stories.description:
Rudyard Kipling's Just So Stories for Little Children (1902) -- twelve pourquoi tales explaining how animals got their features and how humanity invented writing, told in Kipling's rolling, rhythmic, endlessly quotable prose. From the Elephant's 'satiable curiosity to the Cat who walks by himself, these are among the most-read-aloud stories in the English language.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2781 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2781).

Note: These stories reflect Kipling's colonial-era worldview and contain descriptions of non-European peoples that reflect period attitudes. The story 'How the Leopard Got His Spots' includes racial characterizations that modern readers should discuss critically.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- Rudyard Kipling (1902, Macmillan) -- Kipling himself illustrated the original edition with distinctive pen-and-ink drawings, each with a detailed caption explaining the illustration
- Joseph M. Gleeson (1912, Doubleday) -- color plate illustrations for the American edition

kalevala.name: The Kalevala
kalevala.description:
The Kalevala, the Finnish national epic compiled by Elias Lonnrot from oral folk poetry (1835/1849), in the English translation by John Martin Crawford (1888). Fifty runos (cantos) of creation myth, shamanic quest, heroic adventure, and cosmic drama — featuring Wainamoinen the eternal singer, Ilmarinen the divine smith, Lemminkainen the reckless hero, and the mysterious Sampo. The foundational text of Finnish identity and a primary inspiration for Tolkien's mythology.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #5186 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5186)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Akseli Gallen-Kallela's Kalevala paintings (1890s-1900s) — iconic Finnish national romantic art depicting key scenes. Joseph Alanen's Kalevala illustrations (1919). A.W. Finch and other Finnish Golden Age artists' Kalevala works.

kids-constellation.name: Kids Constellation: Stories, Storyboards & Media
kids-constellation.description: A map of maps for the children's channel. 64 grammars of stories, mythology, storyboards, and media. From PlayfulProcess originals (Shiryo, Story Tree, Alice chapter books) to public domain classics (Pinocchio, Peter Pan, world mythology) to curated video playlists (PBS Kids, Art with Mati and Dada). Each item is a doorway into a full grammar.

kids-workouts-gvo-kids-youtube-playlist.name: Kids Workouts - GVO Kids YouTube Playlist
kids-workouts-gvo-kids-youtube-playlist.description: Fun workouts for the whole family.

king-arthur-kids.name: Sword and Grail: King Arthur for Bedtime
king-arthur-kids.description:
Arthurian legend retold as shamanic mythology for bedtime. 42 stories in rhyming verse with an esoteric, Celtic lens — Merlin as the last Druid, the Grail as vessel of divine consciousness, Avalon as the Otherworld, the Round Table as mandala. Designed for 5-minute bedtime reading with karaoke-style TTS and full-bleed illustrations.

Source: Original retellings from memory, drawing on Malory's Le Morte d'Arthur (Project Gutenberg eBook #1251, https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1251), Chrétien de Troyes, Wolfram von Eschenbach, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, and Geoffrey of Monmouth.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Aubrey Beardsley's Art Nouveau illustrations for Le Morte d'Arthur (J.M. Dent, 1893-94) — ~350 stunning black-and-white drawings with sinuous lines and decorative borders. Howard Pyle's pen-and-ink illustrations for The Story of King Arthur and His Knights (Scribner's, 1903) — heroic Golden Age style. Walter Crane's color plates for King Arthur's Knights (T.C. & E.C. Jack, 1911) — Arts & Crafts tapestry style. Edward Burne-Jones Pre-Raphaelite paintings (1881-1898) — The Last Sleep of Arthur in Avalon, The Beguiling of Merlin, Holy Grail tapestries.

king-arthur-kids-storyboard.name: Sword and Grail: King Arthur for Bedtime — Storyboard
king-arthur-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on Sword and Grail: King Arthur for Bedtime. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

kybalion.name: The Kybalion
kybalion.description:
The Kybalion by Three Initiates (1912) — a study of Hermetic philosophy covering the seven Hermetic principles: Mentalism, Correspondence, Vibration, Polarity, Rhythm, Cause and Effect, and Gender. Originally published by The Yogi Publication Society.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #14209 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/14209)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Hermetic and alchemical emblems from works such as the Mutus Liber (1677), Atalanta Fugiens by Michael Maier (1618), and the Rosarium Philosophorum (16th century) — rich symbolic imagery associated with the Hermetic tradition.

leaves-of-grass.name: Leaves of Grass
leaves-of-grass.description:
Walt Whitman's Leaves of Grass — the complete "deathbed" edition of 1891-92, containing nearly 400 poems spanning four decades of work. From the cosmic ecstasy of "Song of Myself" to the Civil War witness of "Drum-Taps" to the luminous late poems of "Sands at Seventy," this is the foundational text of American poetry and one of the great sacred books of democratic civilization. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1322 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1322).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Thomas Eakins' 1887 portrait of Whitman (oil on canvas, Pennsylvania Academy of Fine Arts) — intimate, luminous portrait of the poet in old age. Samuel Hollyer's 1855 engraving of Whitman (frontispiece to the first edition) — the young Whitman in workman's clothes, hat cocked. George C. Cox's 1887 photographs of Whitman — multiple poses showing the "Good Gray Poet" in his Camden years. Frederick Gutekunst's 1889 photograph — the iconic white-bearded profile.

legends-alhambra.name: The Alhambra
legends-alhambra.description:
Washington Irving's The Alhambra (1832, revised 1851) -- the book that introduced the English-speaking world to the enchantment of Moorish Spain. Part travel journal, part historical chronicle, part fairy-tale collection, it records Irving's months living in the crumbling palace of the Alhambra in Granada, exploring its halls, befriending its inhabitants, and gathering the legends that haunt its walls. 41 chapters of travel writing, historical sketches, and legends of enchanted treasure, imprisoned princesses, spectral soldiers, and the last Moorish king.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #49947 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/49947)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Joseph Pennell's illustrations for the 1896 Macmillan edition of The Alhambra. George Cattermole's illustrations for earlier editions. Photographs and engravings of the Alhambra from the 19th century, including those by Charles Clifford (1850s-60s). David Roberts' lithographs of Spanish and Moorish architecture (1830s-40s). Gustave Doré's engravings of Spain (1874).

legends-of-gods-egyptian.name: Legends of the Gods
legends-of-gods-egyptian.description:
E.A. Wallis Budge's collection of the major legends of the Egyptian gods, with translations from the original hieroglyphic and hieratic texts. Includes the Legend of Creation, the Destruction of Mankind, Ra and Isis, Horus and the Winged Disk, the Birth and Death of Horus, the legends of Khensu and Khnemu, and Plutarch's complete narrative of Isis and Osiris. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #9411 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/9411)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The plates from the original 1912 edition by Budge, including Horus spearing the Hippopotamus-fiend, the Procreation of Horus, the Resurrection of Osiris, and the Bekhten and Metternich Steles. Also: reliefs from the Temple of Edfu depicting the Horus myths.

life-of-the-bee.name: The Life of the Bee
life-of-the-bee.description:
Maurice Maeterlinck's The Life of the Bee (1901) — a philosophical meditation on the honeybee colony that blends natural history, philosophy, and prose poetry. Maeterlinck describes the architecture of the comb, the drama of the swarm, the succession of queens, the nuptial flight, and the massacre of the drones, asking throughout what the 'spirit of the hive' can teach us about consciousness, cooperation, and the meaning of life. Translated by Alfred Sutro.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #4511 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/4511)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: E.A. Butler's illustrations of bee anatomy and behavior. Karl von Frisch's early bee research diagrams. Botanical illustrations of bee-pollinated flowers by Pierre-Joseph Redoute (1759-1840). Natural history plates from 19th-century entomological works.

luiza-lian.name: Luiza Lian: Cartografia do Sagrado
luiza-lian.description:
Luiza Lian is a São Paulo artist whose music braids MPB, electronic production, and Afro-Brazilian spirituality into a living cartography of the sacred. Across four albums — her self-titled debut (2016), Oyá Tempo (2017), Azul Moderno (2018), and 7 Estrelas | quem arrancou o céu? (2023) — she maps where the orixás live inside modern Brazilian life: in crowded buses, empty refrigerators, buried rivers, and torn skies.

She is not performing religion. She is channeling it — the way a river channels rain.

This grammar holds each song as a card. Portuguese lyrics sit beside Yoruba English translations — not word-for-word but spirit-for-spirit transmissions that carry cultural and spiritual context across languages. The meta-emergences reveal deeper constellations: the Yabás (feminine orixás), Sacred Waters (Oxum & Yemanjá), the Crossroads (Exu & Pomba Gira), and Storms of Transformation (Oyá/Iansã).

Produced by Charles Tixier. Co-produced by Tim Bernardes (O Terno). Released on selo RISCO.

mabinogion.name: The Mabinogion
mabinogion.description:
The great collection of Welsh mythology and romance — twelve tales drawn from the Red Book of Hergest and the White Book of Rhydderch (13th-14th century manuscripts preserving much older oral traditions). Contains the Four Branches of the Mabinogi (the oldest Welsh mythic cycle), the Welsh Arthurian romances (including the earliest prose Grail story), and the tale of Taliesin's transformation. The gateway to the Celtic Otherworld.

Source: Project Gutenberg. Lady Charlotte Guest translation (1838-1849).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Alan Lee's Mabinogion illustrations (check copyright). Arthur Rackham's Celtic mythology illustrations. Edward Burne-Jones's tapestries of the Holy Grail. Medieval Welsh manuscript illuminations from the Red Book of Hergest.

mahabharata.name: The Mahabharata (Volume 1: Books 1-3)
mahabharata.description:
The Mahabharata of Krishna-Dwaipayana Vyasa, Volume 1, in the English prose translation of Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896). This volume contains the first three of the epic's eighteen books: Adi Parva (The Book of the Beginning), Sabha Parva (The Book of the Assembly Hall), and Vana Parva (The Book of the Forest). Together they tell the origin of the Bharata dynasty, the birth of the Pandavas and Kauravas, the fateful dice game, and the twelve-year forest exile — the great arc from which the rest of the epic unfolds. The Mahabharata is the longest epic poem in the world, a vast ocean of stories containing philosophy, dharma, mythology, and the human condition in all its complexity.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #15474 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/15474)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Indian miniature paintings of Mahabharata scenes from Mughal and Rajput traditions (16th-18th centuries). Raja Ravi Varma's paintings of Draupadi, Arjuna, and other characters (1890s). Illustrations from early 20th century editions published by the Bharata Karyalaya.

maya-kids.name: The Ballcourt of the Gods: Stories from the Maya
maya-kids.description:
K'iche' Maya mythology in rhyming verse for bedtime. The silence before creation, the false sun's vanity, the first ballplayers who descended to the underworld, and the Hero Twins who defeated the lords of death not with weapons but with TRICKS — and then became the sun and the moon so that humans could be made from CORN.

The Popol Vuh is a survival document. When Spanish colonizers burned Maya books in the 1500s, anonymous K'iche' scribes wrote down their sacred stories in Latin script to preserve them. Every story in this grammar exists because someone refused to let their world be erased. The Hero Twins — Hunahpu and Xbalanque — are perhaps the most creative heroes in all world mythology: they defeat death by dying on purpose, coming back as catfish, then as wandering performers, tricking the lords of Xibalba into asking to be killed. No swords. No armies. Just cleverness, sacrifice, and corn.

Source: Dennis Tedlock translation (1996), Allen J. Christenson translation (2007). Based on the K'iche' Maya manuscript preserved in the Newberry Library, Chicago.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Maya codex pages — the Dresden Codex (c. 13th century, celestial tables and deity figures), Madrid Codex (rituals and daily life), Paris Codex (zodiacal pages). Frederick Catherwood (1799-1854) — extraordinary detailed lithographs of Maya ruins from Incidents of Travel in Central America, Chiapas, and Yucatan (1841) with John Lloyd Stephens. Maya ceramic rollout photographs — painted vessels depicting the Hero Twins, ballgame scenes, and Xibalba lords. Diego Rivera (1886-1957) — murals in the Palacio Nacional depicting pre-Columbian Mesoamerican civilization (note: Rivera's work enters public domain 70 years after death, check jurisdiction).

maya-kids-storyboard.name: The Ballcourt of the Gods: Stories from the Maya — Storyboard
maya-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on The Ballcourt of the Gods: Stories from the Maya. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

meditations-marcus-aurelius.name: Meditations — Marcus Aurelius
meditations-marcus-aurelius.description:
The private philosophical journal of Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius (121-180 CE), written during military campaigns on the Danube frontier. Twelve books of Stoic reflections on duty, impermanence, virtue, and the nature of the universe. Never intended for publication, these meditations are the most intimate surviving document from the ancient world — a philosopher-king talking to himself about how to live. Meric Casaubon translation (1634), the first English edition. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2680 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2680).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Busts and statues of Marcus Aurelius — the equestrian statue in the Capitoline Museums (Rome), numerous marble busts. Eugène Delacroix's 'Marcus Aurelius' paintings (19th century). Ancient Roman reliefs from the Column of Marcus Aurelius (Piazza Colonna, Rome) — battle scenes and imperial ceremonies. Illustrations from early printed editions of the Meditations (17th-18th century frontispieces). Neoclassical paintings of Stoic philosophers by Jacques-Louis David and others.

meister-eckhart-sermons.name: The Cell of Self-Knowledge
meister-eckhart-sermons.description:
Seven early English mystical treatises from the golden age of medieval contemplative writing (late 14th century), compiled by Henry Pepwell in 1521 and edited by Edmund G. Gardner in 1910. The collection includes works by or inspired by Richard of St. Victor, St. Catherine of Siena, Margery Kempe, Walter Hilton, and the anonymous author of the Cloud of Unknowing. Together they form a complete curriculum in medieval contemplative practice: from the systematic psychology of contemplation (Benjamin) through the passionate devotion of the women mystics, to the practical guidance on prayer and discernment from the Cloud school. The title comes from Catherine of Siena: 'Let us stay in the cell of self-knowledge.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #4544 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/4544)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Medieval manuscript illuminations from English and Italian sources. Fra Angelico's contemplative scenes. Giovanni di Paolo's illustrations of Catherine of Siena. Medieval anchoress cells and scriptorium scenes.

metamorphoses-ovid.name: Ovid's Metamorphoses
metamorphoses-ovid.description:
The Metamorphoses of Ovid (Books I-VII), the great Roman poem of transformation, rendered into English prose by Henry T. Riley (1851). Spanning from the creation of the world out of Chaos to the magical flights of Medea, these 66 myths form a continuous river of change: gods become animals, mortals become trees, love becomes loss, and grief becomes landscape. Every story is a metamorphosis -- and every metamorphosis asks what it means to become something other than what you were. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #21765 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/21765).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's illustrations for 'Tales from Shakespeare' and mythological subjects (early 1900s) -- atmospheric watercolors of classical scenes. John William Waterhouse's paintings of mythological subjects (1880s-1910s) -- Pre-Raphaelite oil paintings of Apollo, Narcissus, Hylas, etc. Gustave Dore's illustrations for classical texts -- dramatic engravings. Walter Crane's illustrations for classical mythology (1890s) -- decorative Arts and Crafts style. Virgil Solis woodcuts for the 1563 Frankfurt edition of the Metamorphoses -- the earliest major illustrated edition, 178 woodcuts, public domain. Bernard Salomon's woodcuts for the 1557 Lyon Metamorphoses -- elegant Renaissance illustrations, public domain.

middlemarch.name: Middlemarch
middlemarch.description:
George Eliot's Middlemarch (1871-72) — widely considered the greatest English novel. Set in a fictional Midlands town during the Reform Bill era (1829-1832), it weaves together the stories of Dorothea Brooke, the ambitious physician Lydgate, the feckless Fred Vincy, and the haunted banker Bulstrode into a comprehensive vision of provincial life. Eighty-six chapters across eight named books, framed by a Prelude invoking Saint Theresa and a Finale meditating on 'unhistoric acts.' Virginia Woolf called it 'one of the few English novels written for grown-up people.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #145 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/145)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: W. E. F. Britten's illustrations for the 1910 Everyman's Library edition of Middlemarch. Hugh Thomson's decorative headpieces. Photographs and engravings of Coventry and Warwickshire (Eliot's model for Middlemarch) from the 1830s-1870s. George Richmond's chalk portrait of George Eliot (1849).

mirror-of-alchimy.name: The Mirror of Alchimy
mirror-of-alchimy.description:
The Mirror of Alchimy (1597 London edition) — a collection of five alchemical treatises: Roger Bacon's Mirror of Alchimy on the transmutation of metals, the Smaragdine (Emerald) Table of Hermes Trismegistus, Hortulanus's Commentary on the Emerald Tablet, Calid's Book of the Secrets of Alchimie, and Bacon's Discourse on the Admirable Force and Efficacy of Art and Nature.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #58393 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/58393)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Woodcuts from 16th-century alchemical editions, engravings from the Musaeum Hermeticum (1678), illustrations from Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens (1618), and the printer's device from the 1597 Richard Olive edition.

modern-dance-breaking-ground.name: Modern Dance: Breaking Ground
modern-dance-breaking-ground.description:
Modern dance begins with a refusal — Isadora Duncan takes off her shoes and corset. What follows is 120 years of artists inventing new ways to move, each generation rebelling against the last while building on it. This grammar maps the pioneers (Duncan, Graham, Cunningham), the lineage chains that connect them, and the meta-movements (liberation, system-building, system-demolishing, synthesis) that give the story its shape.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of Isadora Duncan by Arnold Genthe (1910s-1920s). Edward Steichen photographs of Martha Graham. Bauhaus stage design sketches by Oskar Schlemmer. Ruth St. Denis photographs in orientalist costume (early 1900s).

movement-culture.name: Movement Culture: The Generalist Body
movement-culture.description:
What if instead of specializing in one movement form, you practiced them all? Movement Culture — popularized by Ido Portal but rooted in older traditions — asks the body to crawl, climb, hang, fight, dance, play, balance, and flow. This grammar maps the movement capacities (what the body can do), the source traditions (where the practices come from), and the philosophy (why generalism matters). Not a style, but a practice of practices.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Eadweard Muybridge, *Animal Locomotion* (1887) — motion studies of human movement. Leonardo da Vinci's *Vitruvian Man* and anatomical studies. Georges Hébert's *La Méthode Naturelle* photographs (1910s-1920s). Greek Olympic athlete sculptures and vase paintings.

mutual-aid.name: Mutual Aid: A Factor of Evolution
mutual-aid.description:
Peter Kropotkin's Mutual Aid: A Factor of Evolution (1902) -- the foundational text of cooperative evolutionary theory. Against Social Darwinism's 'survival of the fittest' as justification for competitive individualism, Kropotkin marshals evidence from zoology, anthropology, and history to show that mutual aid -- not mutual struggle -- is the chief factor in evolution and civilization. From Siberian field observations to medieval guild records, this is the scientific case for cooperation as the natural state of life.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #4341 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/4341)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Natural history illustrations from the 19th century. Ernst Haeckel's Art Forms in Nature. Medieval guild and city illustrations from illuminated manuscripts.

my-first-summer-in-sierra.name: My First Summer in the Sierra
my-first-summer-in-sierra.description:
John Muir's My First Summer in the Sierra (1911) — the ecstatic journal of a young naturalist's first season in the mountains of California. Based on Muir's 1869 journals, the book follows his ascent from the Central Valley through the forest belts to the high granite peaks of Yosemite and the Tuolumne. 11 chapters of precise observation and rapturous celebration that helped inspire the American conservation movement.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #32540 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/32540)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Herbert W. Gleason's photographs of the Sierra Nevada (early 1900s), which accompanied the original edition. Charles S. Olcott's Yosemite photographs. William Keith's paintings of the Sierra (Keith was Muir's close friend). Carleton Watkins' and Eadweard Muybridge's photographs of Yosemite (1860s-1870s).

myth-birth-hero-rank.name: The Myth of the Birth of the Hero
myth-birth-hero-rank.description:
Otto Rank's groundbreaking 1914 study comparing fifteen hero birth myths across world cultures — from Sargon of Akkad to Lohengrin. Each hero follows the same pattern: distinguished parentage, prophecy of danger, exposure as an infant, rescue by humble folk, discovery of true identity, and rise to greatness. Rank's psychoanalytic interpretation sees the myth as the child's universal fantasy of secret royal origins — the 'family romance' that Freud identified.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #66192 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/66192).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Classical depictions of hero myths from Greek vase paintings. Gustave Doré's illustrations of Moses. Medieval manuscript illustrations of Siegfried, Tristan, and Lohengrin. Babylonian cylinder seals depicting Sargon and Gilgamesh.

myth-greece-rome.name: Myths of Greece and Rome
myth-greece-rome.description:
Otto Seemann's comprehensive guide to the mythology of Greece and Rome, with special reference to its use in art. Covers the entire pantheon from the Olympian gods through sea deities, earth spirits, and underworld powers, plus the great heroic legends from Heracles to the Trojan War.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #61901 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/61901)

myths-babylonia-assyria.name: Myths of Babylonia and Assyria
myths-babylonia-assyria.description:
Donald A. Mackenzie's comprehensive survey of Mesopotamian mythology and history (1915) — from the creation epic Enuma Elish and the descent of Ishtar to the fall of Babylon. Covers the gods (Ea, Enlil, Marduk, Ishtar, Tammuz), the epic of Gilgamesh, Babylonian astrology, and three millennia of empire.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #16653 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/16653).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Mesopotamian cylinder seal impressions from the British Museum collections. Austen Henry Layard's drawings of Assyrian palace reliefs (1849). Bas-reliefs from Nineveh showing winged bulls (lamassu) and battle scenes.

myths-china-japan.name: Myths of China and Japan
myths-china-japan.description:
Donald A. Mackenzie's survey of East Asian mythology (1923) — Chinese dragon lore, creation myths of Pangu and Nüwa, Taoist immortals, Japanese kami, the sun goddess Amaterasu, and the hero Yamato-takeru. 21 chapters covering the mythological traditions of China and Japan.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #67344 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/67344).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese dragon paintings from the Ming and Qing dynasties. Ukiyo-e prints by Hokusai and Hiroshige depicting Japanese mythology. Shinto shrine art depicting Amaterasu emerging from the cave.

myths-legends-egypt-spence.name: Myths and Legends of Ancient Egypt
myths-legends-egypt-spence.description:
Lewis Spence's survey of Egyptian mythology, religion, magic, and culture (1915). Nine chapters covering the gods, the Osirian mysteries, temple rites, magical practices, and Egyptian art — a comprehensive introduction to the sacred world of the Nile Valley.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #43662 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/43662)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Evelyn Paul — colour plates for the 1915 David D. Nickerson & Company edition. Also: Egyptian tomb paintings, papyrus illustrations (Book of the Dead), and temple reliefs from Karnak, Luxor, and Abu Simbel.

myths-legends-great-plains.name: Myths and Legends of the Great Plains
myths-legends-great-plains.description:
Seventy-two myths, legends, songs, and sacred traditions from the Great Plains Indian nations — Osage, Cherokee, Omaha, Dakota, Teton, Pawnee, Comanche, Arikara, Mandan, Ponca, Kiowa, Kansa, Delaware, Yankton, Arapahoe, and Assiniboin. Selected and edited by Katharine Berry Judson (1913) from the fieldwork of the Bureau of American Ethnology. These texts encompass creation stories, buffalo ceremonials, trickster tales, ghost encounters, sacred songs, and origin myths, preserving the living cosmology of peoples who dwelt in intimate relationship with the vast prairies, thunderstorms, and buffalo herds of the American heartland. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #22083 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/22083).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: George Catlin's paintings of Plains Indian life (1830s-1840s, Smithsonian American Art Museum) — portraits, ceremonies, and landscapes. Karl Bodmer's aquatint illustrations from 'Travels in the Interior of North America' (1832-1834) — detailed depictions of Mandan, Sioux, and other Plains peoples. Edward S. Curtis photographs (early 1900s, Library of Congress) — portraits and ceremonial scenes.

myths-north-american-indians.name: The Myths of the North American Indians
myths-north-american-indians.description:
A comprehensive collection of myths and legends from the major North American Indian tribal groups — Algonquian, Iroquois, Sioux, Pawnee, and the Northern and North-Western peoples — as recorded and retold by Lewis Spence (1914). Features the trickster-creator Glooskap, the Thunder-god Hi'nun, the dark trickster Ictinike, sacred ceremonial traditions, and the extraordinary mythology of the Pacific Northwest. Spence draws on the fieldwork of the Bureau of American Ethnology to present these myths as living spiritual traditions of extraordinary depth and beauty. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #42390 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/42390).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: James Jack's original 32 colour plates for the 1914 George G. Harrap edition — vivid paintings of mythological scenes. George Catlin's paintings of Native American life and ceremony (1830s-1840s). Karl Bodmer's illustrations from 'Travels in the Interior of North America' (1832-1834). Edward S. Curtis photographs of Native American peoples (early 1900s, Library of Congress).

myths-through-many-eyes.name: Myths Through Many Eyes
myths-through-many-eyes.description:
Forty-three world myths told in their own voice, then seen through the eyes of thirteen interpreters — Jung, Campbell, Frazer, the alchemists, Estés, Warner, Akomolafe, Shaw, and others. The myths come first — as living stories, not evidence for anyone's theory. Then the interpreters emerge as readers, each seeing different patterns. Thematic threads (The Descent, The Dying God, The Sacred Marriage, The Trickster) weave across cultures. The grammar inverts the usual hierarchy: the stories are primary, the theories are emergent.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Greek vase paintings (6th-5th century BCE) for Greek myths. Egyptian tomb paintings and papyrus illustrations from the Book of the Dead. Mesopotamian cylinder seal impressions for Gilgamesh and Inanna. Medieval alchemical woodcuts from Rosarium Philosophorum (1550) and Atalanta Fugiens (1617). Arthur Rackham's mythology illustrations (1900s-1920s). Gustave Doré's engravings. William Blake's mythological paintings.

nastacia-conta.name: Nastácia Conta — She Was Always the Author
nastacia-conta.description:
A retelling of Monteiro Lobato's entire Sítio do Picapau Amarelo universe through the eyes of Tia Nastácia — the Black woman Lobato wrote as a cook, but who was always the keeper of the stories, the herbs, the prayers, and the children. Each item is a book or episode from Lobato's library, told the way Nastácia lived it: what she was thinking in the kitchen while adventures happened in the yard, what she prayed for at night, which spirits she spoke to in the forest, what she really felt about the family she served and the children she loved more than their parents understood.

Nastácia carries Yoruba ancestry. She goes to church on Sunday and bate a cabeça — but not because she reveres the Christian idols above. Her gods are buried beneath the church floor. She placed them there herself. Every time she bows, she reciprocates with the animist forces her ancestors were faithful to. She found her way to keep faith even in the middle of colonial erasure.

Written simply, so older children can read it and begin to learn about the Yoruba spiritual world that survived slavery, syncretism, and a century of children's books that made the cook invisible.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura) from J. Borges and other northeastern Brazilian artists. Percy Lau's 'Tipos e Aspectos do Brasil' illustrations of Brazilian folk life (1940s IBGE series). Pierre Verger's photographs of Candomblé and Umbanda ceremonies (selected public domain images, 1940s-50s). Tarsila do Amaral's modernist paintings of rural Brazilian life (works entering public domain). Jean-Baptiste Debret's illustrations of Black life in colonial Brazil (1834-1839, public domain).

national-geographic-tucci-in-italy.name: National Geographic: Tucci in Italy
national-geographic-tucci-in-italy.description: Ahead of the 2026 Milano-Cortina Olympics, Stanley Tucci and National Geographic explore the pioneering spirit of Italy’s industrial heartland, the Lombardy region, where the cuisine and ingredients are defiantly forward-thinking. Stanley Tucci tries vegan food at Autogrill, explores a caviar farm, and makes gnocchi with a family navigating adoption policies. And more!

natural-supernatural-things.name: Of Natural and Supernatural Things
natural-supernatural-things.description:
Of Natural and Supernatural Things by Basilius Valentinus (c. 15th century), translated by Daniel Cable (1671) — a treatise on the first tincture, root, and spirit of metals and minerals, with supplementary works by Roger Bacon on Antimony and John Isaac Holland on Saturn.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #26340 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/26340)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Woodcuts and engravings from Basil Valentine's Twelve Keys (Musaeum Hermeticum, 1678), the alchemical illustrations of the Splendor Solis (c. 1582), and planetary metal symbols from Athanasius Kircher's works.

new-light-alchymie.name: A New Light of Alchymie
new-light-alchymie.description:
A landmark 1650 compilation of alchemical philosophy translated by John French, M.D., bringing together Sendivogius's Twelve Treatises and Treatise of Sulphur, Paracelsus's Nine Books of the Nature of Things, and a comprehensive Chymicall Dictionary. Covers the full spectrum of Renaissance alchemical thought: the nature of matter, the four elements, the three principles (Mercury, Sulphur, Salt), the generation and transmutation of metals, the Philosophers' Stone, and the Doctrine of Signatures.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #61112 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/61112)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Alchemical woodcuts and engravings from 17th-century editions; Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens (1618) emblems; Heinrich Khunrath's Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae (1595) engravings; illustrations from Musaeum Hermeticum (1678).

norse-kids.name: The World Tree: Stories from the North
norse-kids.description:
Forty-two Norse myths retold as rhyming bedtime verses for children, spanning from the void of Ginnungagap to the green world reborn after Ragnarök. This is the ONLY major mythology where the gods LOSE — Odin falls, Thor dies, the world burns — and then it begins again, green and new. That cycle of ending-and-beginning makes Norse myth uniquely honest about loss and uniquely hopeful about renewal.

Sources: Snorri Sturluson's Prose Edda (c. 1220), the Poetic Edda / Elder Edda (13th century Codex Regius), and R.B. Anderson's Norse Mythology (1875).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: W.G. Collingwood's illustrations for the Prose Edda translation by Olive Bray (1908) — stark black-and-white ink drawings of Odin, Thor, and the World Tree. Arthur Rackham's Ring Cycle illustrations for Wagner (1910-1911) — atmospheric watercolors of gods, giants, and twilight. Lorenz Frølich's Norse mythology illustrations (1895) — detailed engravings of Eddic scenes. Viking Age carvings and runestones, especially the Gotland picture stones depicting Odin's horse Sleipnir and scenes from Ragnarök.

norse-kids-storyboard.name: The World Tree: Stories from the North — Storyboard
norse-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on The World Tree: Stories from the North. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

olaudah-equiano.name: The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano
olaudah-equiano.description:
Olaudah Equiano's 'The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano, or Gustavus Vassa, the African, Written by Himself' (1789) — one of the earliest and most influential slave narratives ever published. Born in what is now Nigeria, Equiano was kidnapped at age eleven, survived the Middle Passage, served in the Royal Navy, purchased his own freedom, and became a leading voice in the British abolitionist movement. His Narrative is at once ethnography of Igbo society, testimony of the slave trade's horrors, adventure story, spiritual autobiography, and political argument for abolition.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #15399 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/15399)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Frontispiece portrait of Olaudah Equiano from the 1789 first edition. Illustrations of slave ships and the Middle Passage from 18th-century abolitionist publications. Maps of the Atlantic slave trade routes. Engravings from the period depicting West African life.

on-liberty.name: On Liberty
on-liberty.description:
John Stuart Mill's On Liberty (1859) -- the foundational text of modern liberalism. Mill argues that individual freedom is not merely a political right but the essential condition of human flourishing. His 'one very simple principle' -- that the only legitimate purpose of power over the individual is to prevent harm to others -- defines the boundary between personal autonomy and social control. His defense of free speech, eccentricity, and 'experiments of living' remains the intellectual arsenal of everyone who believes that society has no business telling individuals how to live.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #34901 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/34901)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Victorian-era portraits and engravings. George Frederic Watts's portrait of Mill (1873). 19th century parliamentary and political illustrations.

one-story-kids.name: The One Story: The Pattern Beneath the Myths
one-story-kids.description:
The meta-grammar of the bedtime mythology series. 42 universal archetypes that appear across all 11 traditions — Biblical, Arthurian, Greek, Hindu, Polynesian, Egyptian, Tibetan, West African, Norse, Maya, and Aboriginal Australian.

This grammar doesn't retell the myths — it reveals the PATTERN. Each item is an archetype (The Belly of the Whale, The Theft of Fire, The Sacred Name) that links to specific stories across all source grammars. A child who hears about Hanuman's leap on Tuesday and Maui's fishing-up-the-islands on Wednesday starts to feel the one story underneath.

42 archetypes × ~7 cross-references each = ~300 links weaving the entire series into one recursive web. The number 42 mirrors the 42 L1 stories in each source grammar. The fractal reflects itself.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Gustav Doré (1832-1883) — cross-cultural Bible and mythology illustrations. Arthur Rackham (1867-1939) — dreamlike fairy tale art. Edmund Dulac (1882-1953) — cross-cultural mythological illustrations. William Blake (1757-1827) — visionary archetypal imagery. Hilma af Klint (1862-1944) — abstract spiritual art perfect for universal pattern visualization.

origin-of-species.name: On the Origin of Species — A Naturalist's Oracle
origin-of-species.description:
Charles Darwin's 1859 masterwork, reimagined as a deck of 60+ reflective cards. Each card carries one of Darwin's key arguments alongside a question for contemplation. Draw a card when you need the long view — when you're struggling, adapting, going extinct, or evolving into something new.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1228, First Edition (1859). https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1228

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- Charles Darwin's own sketches and notebooks (1837-1859), including the famous 'I think' tree of life diagram
- Ernst Haeckel, Kunstformen der Natur (1904) — stunning biological illustrations of radiolarians, jellyfish, orchids
- Philip Henry Gosse, Actinologia Britannica (1860) — sea anemones and marine life
- Audubon, Birds of America (1827-1838) — for chapters on island biogeography
- Plates from the Voyage of the Beagle (1839) — landscapes and specimens from Darwin's journey

patchwork-pawls-filmbilder-friends.name: Patchwork Pawls - Filmbilder & Friends
patchwork-pawls-filmbilder-friends.description: From the same creators as AnimaAnimals.

pbs-kids.name: PBS KIDS
pbs-kids.description: Videos form PBS Kids channel in YouTube Kids. Daniel Tiger is the best, but there is more!

peter-pan.name: Peter Pan
peter-pan.description:
J.M. Barrie's Peter Pan (originally published as Peter and Wendy, 1911) — the beloved story of the boy who never grew up, the Darling children's flight to Neverland, and the bittersweet truth that all children, except one, grow up. Structured as scenes within 17 chapters, with thematic groupings for adventure, imagination, growing up, mothers and home, and good and evil.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #16 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/16). Text matches the 1911 original publication.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- F.D. Bedford (1911, Hodder & Stoughton) — original edition illustrator, detailed pen-and-ink drawings of Neverland, the Darling nursery, and the pirate ship
- Arthur Rackham (1906, Hodder & Stoughton) — illustrated Peter Pan in Kensington Gardens, atmospheric watercolors of fairies and the Serpentine
- Mabel Lucie Attwell (1921, Hodder & Stoughton) — charming, rounded children's illustrations

phaedo-plato.name: Phaedo
phaedo-plato.description:
Plato's Phaedo in Benjamin Jowett's translation — Socrates' final conversation before death, on the immortality of the soul. The dialogue presents four arguments for immortality (from opposites, from recollection, from affinity, and from essential nature), interwoven with the dramatic narrative of Socrates' last hours and his cosmological myth of the true earth and the afterlife. Includes Jowett's Introduction.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1658 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1658). Benjamin Jowett translation.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Jacques-Louis David's 'The Death of Socrates' (1787, Metropolitan Museum of Art). Ancient Greek symposium scenes from Attic red-figure pottery. Raphael's 'School of Athens' (1509-1511, Vatican).

phaedrus-plato.name: Phaedrus
phaedrus-plato.description:
Plato's dialogue on love, beauty, the soul, and rhetoric -- one of the most lyrical works in Western philosophy. Socrates and Phaedrus walk along the river Ilissus, deliver speeches on the nature of love, explore the myth of the winged soul, and debate whether writing can transmit genuine wisdom. Translated by Benjamin Jowett.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1636 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1636)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Greek vase paintings depicting Eros and the soul; William Blake's illustrations of divine vision and the human form; Gustave Moreau's mythological paintings of divine love and madness (1880s); John Flaxman's neoclassical line illustrations of Greek philosophy (1793).

philippine-folk-tales.name: Philippine Folk Tales
philippine-folk-tales.description:
Philippine Folk Tales, compiled and annotated by Mabel Cook Cole (1916), from four years of fieldwork among the tribes of the Philippine Islands. Sixty-two tales from five cultural groups spanning creation myths, shamanic epics, hero legends, and animal fables — the Tinguian heroes of the 'first times,' the Igorot creator-spirit Lumawig, the Moro prince Bantugan, and the trickster animal tales of the coastal peoples. One of the most important early collections of Philippine oral literature.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #12814 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/12814)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The original 1916 A.C. McClurg edition includes photographs of Philippine tribal life. Dean C. Worcester's photographs of Philippine peoples (1898-1914, National Geographic). Charles Martin's hand-tinted photographs of the Philippines (early 1900s, National Geographic).

pistis-sophia.name: Pistis Sophia
pistis-sophia.description:
The most complete Gnostic scripture — a Coptic text recording the resurrected Jesus teaching his disciples the mysteries of the cosmos. At its center: the myth of Sophia (Wisdom), who falls from the light, is trapped by archons, and cries out in thirteen repentances until rescued by Jesus. Five documents covering the drama of the fallen divine feminine, the mysteries of baptism and salvation, and the fate of souls after death.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #76266 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/76266).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Gnostic gems and amulets from the British Museum. William Blake's visionary paintings (particularly 'The Ancient of Days' and 'Satan in his Original Glory'). Byzantine icons of Christ Pantocrator and Sophia/Wisdom.

playful-process-the-path-of-the-ontoject.name: Playful Process: The Path of the Ontoject
playful-process-the-path-of-the-ontoject.description: An existential tarot exploring the dance between Being and Non-Being, drawing from Tillich's 'The Courage to Be' and Palmer's 'Let Your Life Speak.' The Ontoject—a conscious spark of awareness—journeys through unity, differentiation, time, structure, and ultimately returns to harmony. This deck invites you into the rhythm of becoming: not as toil, but as delight.

plotinus-enneads.name: Plotinus: The Enneads
plotinus-enneads.description:
The complete Enneads of Plotinus, the foundational text of Neoplatonism, translated by Kenneth Sylvan Guthrie. Fifty-four tractates organized in six groups of nine ('enneads'), covering metaphysics, ethics, cosmology, and the nature of reality. Plotinus's vision of the One, Nous, and Soul shaped Western mysticism, theology, and philosophy for nearly two millennia.

Source: Project Gutenberg

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Raphael's 'School of Athens' (1509-1511) includes idealized portraits of ancient philosophers. Ancient Roman busts and mosaics depicting philosophers.

plutarchs-morals.name: Plutarch's Moralia
plutarchs-morals.description:
Plutarch's moral essays — practical wisdom on education, marriage, anger, friendship, fortune, and the cultivation of character. These essays reveal the humane, psychologically astute side of one of antiquity's greatest writers.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #23639 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23639)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Ancient Roman portraits and busts. Illustrations from 19th century editions of Plutarch's works.

plutchik-wheel-of-emotions.name: Plutchik's Wheel of Emotions: Myths for Every Feeling
plutchik-wheel-of-emotions.description:
Robert Plutchik's Wheel of Emotions rendered as a mythic journey. Eight primary emotions — each at three intensities (petal, core, deep) — plus eight composite 'dyad' emotions formed by adjacent pairs. Every emotion is a world myth or folktale that Alice, Shiryo, and Bishisheshi jump into and live through. The wheel becomes a map: each petal is a story, each story is a feeling, each feeling has a body, a color, and a song.

Designed as a companion to The Wise Heart (DBT grammar) — while DBT teaches skills for handling emotions, this grammar teaches the emotions themselves. You need to know what you're feeling before you can do anything about it.

polynesian-kids.name: The Ocean's Children: Stories from the Pacific
polynesian-kids.description:
Polynesian mythology in rhyming verse for bedtime. The creation of the world from Rangi and Papa's embrace, Maui's impossible feats (fishing up islands, stealing fire, slowing the sun), Pele's volcanic journey across the Pacific, the sacred art of star navigation, the great gods as forces of nature, and the voyaging canoe that carried a civilization across the largest ocean on Earth.

This grammar draws from pan-Polynesian shared traditions — stories told across Māori, Hawaiian, Samoan, and Tongan cultures. Where stories vary between island traditions, we follow the most widely shared version and note the variations in the Whisper sections.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Tapa cloth designs — bold geometric patterns in brown, black, and cream. William Hodges (1744-1797) — Cook expedition artist, luminous Pacific landscapes. John Webber (1751-1793) — Cook expedition portraits and scenes, detailed ethnographic illustrations. Hawaiian petroglyph rubbings — powerful simple line figures. Māori carved meeting house panels (museum photographs) — intricate spiral patterns (koru). European botanical illustrations of Pacific plants (breadfruit, kūmara, taro).

polynesian-kids-storyboard.name: The Ocean's Children: Stories from the Pacific — Storyboard
polynesian-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on The Ocean's Children: Stories from the Pacific. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

popol-vuh.name: The Popol Vuh
popol-vuh.description:
The Popol Vuh ('Book of the Community'), the creation myth of the K'iche' Maya of Central America, retold by Lewis Spence (1908).
