The most complete surviving mythology of pre-Columbian Mesoamerica: the primordial waters, the failed creations of humanity from mud and wood, the Hero Twins' descent into the underworld of Xibalba, and the final creation of true humans from maize. As Spence notes: it is 'the New World's richest mythological mine.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #56550 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/56550)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Maya codex illustrations (Dresden, Madrid, Paris codices). Frederick Catherwood's lithographs of Maya ruins from 'Incidents of Travel in Central America' (1841). Maya ceramic paintings of the Hero Twins.

practice-presence-of-god.name: The Practice of the Presence of God
practice-presence-of-god.description:
Brother Lawrence's Practice of the Presence of God — 4 conversations and 15 letters from a 17th-century Carmelite friar who found God not in grand mystical visions but in washing dishes and repairing sandals. His method was radical simplicity: maintain a continuous, loving awareness of God's presence in every ordinary moment. This is the contemplative root of mindfulness practice in the Christian tradition.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #5657 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5657)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 17th century French monastery paintings. Georges de La Tour's candlelit scenes. Zurbarán's monks in contemplation.

pragmatism.name: Pragmatism
pragmatism.description:
William James's Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking (1907) — the founding text of America's most distinctive philosophical tradition. Eight lectures delivered at the Lowell Institute and Columbia University, presenting pragmatism as both a method for resolving philosophical disputes and a revolutionary theory of truth. James argues that the meaning of any concept lies in its practical consequences, and that truth is not a fixed correspondence but a living, growing process of verification. Warm, witty, and devastatingly concrete.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #5116 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5116)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of William James (various, 1890s-1900s). Images of Harvard University and the Lowell Institute in Boston. Portraits by his wife Alice Howe Gibbens James.

principles-of-psychology.name: Principles of Psychology (Volume 1)
principles-of-psychology.description:
William James's The Principles of Psychology (1890) — the founding text of American psychology. Volume 1 covers sixteen chapters from the scope and methods of psychology through brain function, habit, the stream of consciousness, the self, attention, and memory. James writes with extraordinary clarity and literary brilliance, combining rigorous empirical observation with philosophical depth. His metaphor of consciousness as a 'stream' rather than a 'chain' revolutionized psychology, philosophy, and literature.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #57628 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/57628)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of William James (1890s-1900s). Brain diagrams from the original 1890 Henry Holt edition. Illustrations of experimental apparatus from 19th-century psychology laboratories. Portraits of James at Harvard.

prose-edda.name: The Prose Edda
prose-edda.description:
The Prose Edda (Younger Edda) by Snorri Sturluson (c. 1220), the most complete medieval account of Norse mythology, in the English translation by Rasmus B. Anderson (1901). Includes The Fooling of Gylfe (Gylfaginning) — the full Norse cosmogony from creation to Ragnarok — and Brage's Talk (Bragaraedur) — the myths behind the poetic kennings. The foundational source for Odin, Thor, Loke, Balder, Ragnarok, and the Norse cosmos.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #18947 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/18947)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: W.G. Collingwood's illustrations for the 1908 Norroena Society Edda editions — pen and ink drawings of Norse mythological scenes. Lorenz Frolich's Norse mythology illustrations (1895) — romantic depictions of the Aesir. Arthur Rackham's illustrations for Wagner's Ring cycle (1910-1911), which draw directly on Eddaic sources.

ramayana-kids.name: Rama's Bow: The Ramayana for Bedtime
ramayana-kids.description:
The Ramayana retold as devotional-cosmic mythology for bedtime. 42 stories in rhyming verse with an esoteric, animist lens — Rama as Vishnu choosing human limitation, Sita as Earth goddess, Hanuman as bhakti made flesh, Ravana as the ten-headed ego. Designed for 5-minute bedtime reading with karaoke-style TTS and full-bleed illustrations.

Source: Original retellings from memory, drawing on Ralph Griffith's verse translation of Valmiki's Ramayana (Project Gutenberg eBook #24869, https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/24869).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Raja Ravi Varma's oleographs and paintings (1890s) — the defining visual language of modern Hindu iconography. Balasaheb Pant Pratinidhi's Ramayana paintings (1916) — rich color with emotional depth. Southeast Asian wayang shadow puppets — intricate silhouettes from the Javanese and Balinese traditions. Mughal and Pahari miniatures (16th-19th century) — jewel-toned scenes. Mewar Ramayana manuscript pages (1649-1653) — 400+ illustrated pages.

ramayana-kids-storyboard.name: Rama's Bow: The Ramayana for Bedtime — Storyboard
ramayana-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on Rama's Bow: The Ramayana for Bedtime. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

relationship-cards.name: Relationship Cards — A Couples Oracle
relationship-cards.description:
A 54-card oracle deck for couples based on relationship science. Draw three cards together — The Pattern (what's showing up), The Need (what's underneath), The Move (what you could try) — and use them as a mirror for your relationship. Grounded in Gottman's research on the masters and disasters of love, Sue Johnson's Emotionally Focused Therapy, Bowlby's attachment theory, and Linehan's dialectical thinking. No card is bad. Even contempt is pointing toward what the relationship is starving for.

5 suits: Patterns (the big dynamics), Needs (what's underneath), Repairs (moves you can make), Strengths (what's already alive), and Shadows (the hard truths). Designed to be played weekly as a couple's ritual — draw, discuss, journal, repeat.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Gustav Klimt's 'The Kiss' (1907-1908) and other Secessionist works depicting intimacy and embrace. Edvard Munch's relationship paintings — 'The Dance of Life' (1899-1900), 'Separation' (1896), 'Attraction' (1896). Auguste Rodin sculptures — 'The Kiss' (1882), 'The Cathedral' (hands). Egon Schiele's embrace paintings (1910s). Japanese ukiyo-e depicting couples — Utamaro's bijin-ga. Indian Mughal miniatures of Radha-Krishna embraces. William Blake's illustrations of marriage from 'Songs of Innocence and Experience' (1789-1794).

repair-an-open-source-book.name: The Repair Deck: A Major Arcana for Mending
repair-an-open-source-book.description:
A complete 22-card Major Arcana tarot deck on repair at every scale — couples, communities, cultures, species. Each card maps a therapeutic, spiritual, or cultural voice to a traditional Major Arcana archetype. The Lovers hold Gottman's perpetual problems. The Moon holds Bowlby's attachment cry. Temperance holds Linehan's dialectics. The Devil holds colonial extraction. Draw a card: it reframes your thinking (CBT), invites archetypal dialogue (depth psychology), cracks your certainty (Akomolafean practice), and offers ten lenses — Research, Clinical, Spiritual, Practices, Rituals, Songs, Kids Version, Sci-Fi, Open Source, and Cracks. Designed for use with journaling, expressive writing, and card design on recursive.eco.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Rider-Waite-Smith illustrations by Pamela Colman Smith (1909, US public domain). Tarot de Marseille woodcuts (Nicolas Conver, 1760). William Blake's illuminated prints. Medieval manuscript marginalia from the British Library's digitized collections.

repair-iching.name: The Repair I Ching: A Contemplative Practice
repair-iching.description:
A meta-grammar that weaves the I Ching into the Repair framework. Twelve hexagrams — one for each chapter of 'Repair: An Open Source Book' — chosen not to predict but to reperceive. Each hexagram illuminates a repair theme: the perpetual problem, the attachment cry, the amends, the fire, the practice. This is not a divination grammar. It is a contemplative grammar. Each item invites illustration: find or create an image that embodies the hexagram's repair teaching. The practice of searching for that image IS the practice.

Companion grammars: 'Repair: An Open Source Book' (the full book), 'I Ching — Chinese Original With Brief Translation' (the 64 hexagrams), '易經 · Emergent Structure' (cosmological architecture).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Chinese landscape painting from the Metropolitan Museum CC0 collection. Smithsonian Open Access botanical and geological images. Biodiversity Heritage Library nature illustrations. NASA planetary imagery. Hokusai woodcuts (Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji). Traditional Chinese ink wash painting reproductions.

republic-plato.name: Republic
republic-plato.description:
Plato's Republic in Benjamin Jowett's translation — the foundational text of Western political philosophy. A Socratic dialogue in ten books exploring justice, the ideal state, the tripartite soul, the philosopher-king, the Allegory of the Cave, the theory of Forms, and the Myth of Er. Includes Jowett's Introduction and Analysis.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1497 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1497). Benjamin Jowett translation.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Raphael's 'School of Athens' (1509-1511, Vatican). Ancient Greek symposium scenes from Attic red-figure pottery. William Blake's illustrations of Platonic themes.

revelations-divine-love.name: Revelations of Divine Love
revelations-divine-love.description:
Julian of Norwich's Revelations of Divine Love (c. 1393) -- the earliest surviving book written by a woman in English. On May 13, 1373, during a near-fatal illness, Julian received sixteen visions ('Shewings') of divine love, which she spent the next twenty years meditating upon and writing down. The result is one of the most profound mystical texts in any language: a sustained theology of love as the ground and meaning of all existence, containing the famous assurance 'All shall be well, and all shall be well, and all manner of thing shall be well.' Julian's teachings on divine motherhood, contemplative prayer, and the ultimate redemption of all things remain startlingly relevant.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #52958 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/52958). Grace Warrack edition (1901), from the Sloane MS. in the British Museum.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Medieval illuminated manuscripts of the Norwich school. Images from the Luttrell Psalter (c. 1325-1340). Anchoress cells and medieval church architecture. Phoebe Anna Traquair's title-page design for the 1901 Warrack edition.

rider-waite-smith-tarot.name: Rider–Waite–Smith Archetypal Tarot Deck
rider-waite-smith-tarot.description:
A 78-card tarot deck with archetypal interpretations for each card. This deck presents the classic Rider–Waite–Smith imagery with a focus on insight, reflection, and symbolic meaning instead of fortune-telling predictions.

Source: Public domain - Wikimedia Commons

rights-of-man.name: Rights of Man
rights-of-man.description:
Thomas Paine's Rights of Man (1791-1792) -- the great manifesto of democratic revolution. Written as a reply to Edmund Burke's attack on the French Revolution, it became the most influential argument for natural rights, representative government, and social welfare in the English language. Part I defends the French Revolution and demolishes hereditary monarchy. Part II proposes a comprehensive system of social welfare -- universal education, old-age pensions, child allowances, progressive taxation -- anticipating the welfare state by 150 years. Paine was prosecuted for sedition and burned in effigy; his ideas conquered the world.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #3742 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/3742)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 18th century political engravings and caricatures. James Gillray's satirical prints. George Romney's portrait of Paine. French Revolution illustrations by contemporary artists.

roots-of-frozen-ii.name: Roots of Frozen II — Nordic Mythology, Sámi Culture & Elemental Spirits for Kids
roots-of-frozen-ii.description:
The real mythic, cultural, and musical traditions behind Disney's Frozen II — told for children. Explore the Sámi people's reindeer culture and sacred joik singing, Norse elemental spirits and frost giants, Scandinavian folk magic, the Northern Lights as ancestor spirits, and the deep forest traditions of northern Europe. Each card connects a real cultural tradition to the film moment it inspired.

Sources: Snorri Sturluson's 'Prose Edda' (c. 1220); Asbjørnsen & Moe's 'Norwegian Folktales' (1841-44); Elias Lönnrot's 'Kalevala' (1835/1849); Sámi cultural consultants and oral tradition. All written source texts are public domain.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Theodor Kittelsen's Norwegian fairy tale paintings (1880s-1900s) — haunting trolls, forests, and northern landscapes. John Bauer's Swedish fairy tale illustrations (1907-1915) — ethereal forests, trolls, and princesses. Kay Nielsen's 'East of the Sun and West of the Moon' illustrations (1914) — Art Nouveau Nordic fairy tales. W.G. Collingwood's Norse mythology illustrations (1908) — dramatic depictions of Edda scenes.

roots-of-moana.name: Roots of Moana — Polynesian Mythology & Wayfinding for Kids
roots-of-moana.description:
The real mythic, cultural, and musical traditions behind Disney's Moana — told for children. Explore the demigod Māui's legendary feats, Polynesian star navigation, oceanic animism where every wave and wind has spirit, the sacred art of voyaging canoes, and the music traditions from hula to log drums. Each card connects a real cultural tradition to the film moment it inspired.

Sources: Polynesian oral tradition; George Grey's 'Polynesian Mythology' (1855); Martha Beckwith's 'Hawaiian Mythology' (1940); Johannes C. Andersen's 'Myths and Legends of the Polynesians' (1928). All source texts are public domain.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John La Farge's Samoan and Tahitian paintings (1890s) — luminous tropical landscapes and Pacific Islander portraits. Arman Manookian's Hawaiian paintings (1920s) — bold figurative work of island life. Jacques Arago's voyage illustrations (1817-1820) — early European depictions of Pacific navigation and culture. Illustrations from 'Polynesian Mythology' by George Grey (1855 edition) — line engravings of Māui legends.

rosicrucian-mysteries.name: The Rosicrucian Mysteries
rosicrucian-mysteries.description:
The Rosicrucian Mysteries: An Elementary Exposition of Their Secret Teachings by Max Heindel (1911, Third Edition) — an introduction to the Rosicrucian Order's metaphysical teachings covering the invisible worlds, the constitution of man, and the mysteries of life, death, and rebirth.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #29855 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/29855)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Rosicrucian emblems from the Fama Fraternitatis (1614) and Confessio Fraternitatis (1615), engravings from Robert Fludd's Utriusque Cosmi Historia (1617-1621), and the Rose Cross symbol from various 17th-century Rosicrucian publications.

royal-ballet-and-opera.name: Royal Ballet and Opera
royal-ballet-and-opera.description: Some masterclasses on Opera singing that are fun to watch with the whole family, plus some ballet performances at the end.

russian-folk-tales.name: Russian Folk-Tales
russian-folk-tales.description:
Seventy-three Russian folk tales from the collection of Alexander Afanasyev, translated by Leonard A. Magnus (1915). The wild forest of Russian imagination: Bába Yagá and her hut on chicken legs, enchanted tsareviches, clever fools, heroic bogatyrs, talking animals, and the dead who dance at midnight. Organized into eight thematic groups from supernatural enchantment to animal tales to the cosmic stories of sun, moon, and river.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #62509 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/62509).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Ivan Bilibin's illustrations for Russian fairy tales (1899-1902) — the definitive visual style. Viktor Vasnetsov's paintings of Russian folklore subjects (1880s-1920s). Elena Polenova's folk art illustrations. Palekh miniature lacquer box paintings of folk tale scenes.

sacred-books-east.name: Sacred Books of the East
sacred-books-east.description:
An anthology of sacred literature from the world's Eastern religious traditions, edited by Epiphanius Wilson (1900). Includes Vedic Hymns, Zend-Avesta, Dhammapada, Upanishads, selections from the Koran, and the Life of Buddha.

Source: Project Gutenberg

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Ancient Indian miniatures, Persian manuscript illuminations, Buddhist art from Gandhara and Ajanta, Islamic geometric patterns and calligraphy.

sadhana.name: Sadhana: The Realisation of Life
sadhana.description:
Sadhana: The Realisation of Life by Rabindranath Tagore (1913). Eight essays originally delivered as lectures at Harvard University, exploring the Upanishadic vision of the individual's unity with the Infinite — through love, action, beauty, and direct realisation. A poet-philosopher's bridge between Eastern wisdom and Western thought.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #6842 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/6842)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Tagore's own paintings and drawings — his unique style of fluid, dreamlike figures in ink and watercolor emerged later in life but captures the spirit of Sadhana perfectly. Also: Abanindranath Tagore and the Bengal School of Art (early 20th century), which shared Rabindranath's vision of synthesizing Indian spiritual tradition with modern artistic expression.

secret-garden.name: The Secret Garden
secret-garden.description:
Frances Hodgson Burnett's The Secret Garden (1911) — the story of Mary Lennox, a disagreeable orphan who discovers a locked garden on the Yorkshire moors and, in bringing it back to life, heals herself, a hidden invalid boy, and a broken family. One of the most beloved children's novels ever written, exploring healing through nature, the power of fresh air and growing things, and the magic of believing in life.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #113 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- Maria Louise Kirk (1911, Frederick A. Stokes) — original American edition illustrator, beautiful color plates of Mary in the garden, Dickon with animals, Colin in his wheelchair
- Charles Robinson (1911, Heinemann) — original British edition illustrator, delicate pen-and-ink drawings with Art Nouveau styling
- Jessie Willcox Smith (early 20th century) — celebrated children's illustrator whose style perfectly suits Burnett's world

seven-rays-ancient-roots.name: Seven Rays — Ancient Roots
seven-rays-ancient-roots.description:
The seven rays as a cosmological system, rooted in the Vedic tradition of Surya's seven horses and the universal pattern of sevenfold differentiation found across Hindu, Buddhist, Kabbalistic, and Gnostic traditions. This grammar centers the ancient geometry: seven qualities of the One Light, each governing a plane of existence, their weaving creating the fabric of manifest reality. It is not a personality typing system or a developmental hierarchy — it is a map of how spirit becomes matter and matter returns to spirit.

The seven rays predate any modern interpreter. In the Rig Veda, Surya's chariot is drawn by seven horses whose names describe qualities of solar force. Blavatsky (The Secret Doctrine, 1888) traced the pattern across civilizations. This grammar presents the rays as they appear across traditions — letting the geometric correspondences speak for themselves.

This is the foundational grammar in a planned Seven Rays family (like the I Ching grammar family), with future grammars exploring Bailey's psychological typology, Leadbeater's Master correspondences, and the Vedic source texts directly.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Hindu temple sculptures of Surya's chariot with seven horses (Konark Sun Temple, 13th century). Tibetan Buddhist thangka paintings of the Five Dhyani Buddhas. Kabbalistic Tree of Life diagrams from Athanasius Kircher's Oedipus Aegyptiacus (1652). Robert Fludd's cosmological diagrams from Utriusque Cosmi (1617). Illustrations from Blavatsky's Secret Doctrine (1888). Medieval manuscript illuminations of the seven planetary spheres.

seven-rays-bailey-expressions.name: Seven Rays — Bailey Expressions
seven-rays-bailey-expressions.description:
The seven rays as a psychological and civilizational typology, based on Alice Bailey's system of higher and lower expressions. Where the Ancient Roots grammar looks upward (rays descending through planes of existence), this grammar looks outward: how do the rays show up in human personality, in institutions, in the rise and fall of civilizations? Each ray has a type (who you are when it dominates), a higher expression (the ray awakened), a lower expression (the ray asleep), a shadow (the ray distorted), and a planetary lord.

This is the Bailey lens — one perspective in the Seven Rays grammar family. It should be read alongside the Ancient Roots grammar, not as a replacement for it. Bailey's system is powerful and practical but carries the limitations of its early 20th-century, Western esoteric context. The typology works best as a mirror for self-recognition, not as a box to put people in.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Portraits of the seven planetary gods from Renaissance and Baroque art: Botticelli's Venus, Caravaggio's Jupiter, Velázquez's Mars. Alchemical engravings of the seven metals and their planetary correspondences from Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens (1617). William Blake's visionary figures. Art Nouveau illustrations of personality types. NASA planetary photographs (public domain).

shaiva-tantra-tattvas.name: The 36 Tattvas: A Grammar of Consciousness
shaiva-tantra-tattvas.description:
The 36 tattvas of non-dual Shaiva Tantra — categories of experience through which consciousness knows itself. Not building blocks of a material universe but the structure of how awareness experiences its own self-expression.

Unmeṣa (opening/expansion — consciousness expressing outward, like eyes opening) and nimeṣa (closing/contraction — consciousness limiting itself, like eyes closing) — emanation and contraction — are the pulse of the whole system. Spanda. The vibration by which awareness knows itself as alive. Cast a tattva and ask: am I expanding or contracting? Neither is wrong. Both are the dance.

IMPORTANT: The chakra-element mappings used here are practice conventions, not fixed doctrine. In the tradition, chakras are prescriptive (you install elements into them for specific practices), not descriptive (they don't inherently 'have' elements). A 5-element practice uses 5 chakras; a 7-deity practice uses 7.

Source: Public teachings of Christopher Wallis (Tantra Illuminated podcast, 307 episodes). Rooted in Abhinavagupta's Tantrasāra and the Pratyabhijñā (Recognition) school.

ORACLE USAGE: You are not asking the cards what will happen or what you should do. You are asking: which aspect of my own consciousness is presenting itself for recognition right now? The card is a mirror, not a map. The near enemy of this practice is using it as a substitute for direct experience — drawing a card and thinking 'my problem is an illusion' instead of looking at what is actually happening. The Practice field on each card is the real work: a contemplative inquiry you do with your body and attention, not a concept you file away. Casting this oracle is āṇavopāya (a specific technique). Letting a single insight dissolve something is śāktopāya. If the whole structure falls away in a moment of recognition — that is śāmbhavopāya, which cannot be planned but can be received.

shakespeare-ten-greatest.name: Shakespeare — Ten Greatest Plays
shakespeare-ten-greatest.description: The complete text of ten of Shakespeare's most celebrated plays, structured by act and scene. Level 1 items contain the full original text of each scene. Level 2 items represent acts. Level 3 items represent complete plays with character lists.

shih-king.name: The Shih King (Book of Poetry)
shih-king.description:
The Book of Poetry (Shih King/Shi Jing), one of the Five Classics of Confucianism, translated by James Legge. Contains odes from the 11th to 7th centuries BCE, including folk songs, court hymns, and ritual odes. This edition presents the religious and ceremonial selections from Legge's translation in Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 3 (1879).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #9394 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/9394)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese painting and calligraphy from the Song and Ming dynasties depicting court scenes, ritual ceremonies, and pastoral life would complement these ancient poems.

social-working.name: Social Working: An Analeptic Deck
social-working.description: An analeptic exploration of social working—not social work (the profession), but the verb: what communities, spirits, elements, and all beings have always done. Closing analysis in 'social work as profession' is already colonizing. Decolonizing means putting social work in a social working perspective. L4 'Social Working' contains: L3 'The Violent History' (Chapman & Withers' 40 names from professional social work); L3 'The Partnership Way' (Eisler's insight that the opposite of patriarchy isn't matriarchy but partnership—where ROLES survive, not names); L3 'Wicked History' (cultural memory that dwarfs the standard account—Jane Addams is a flat line on Google Trends while Wicked explodes); L3 'The Rest of the Worlds' (more-than-human social working: kingdoms, elements, epistemologies). All figures are complicit. The mainstream has never run clean.

sojourner-truth.name: Narrative of Sojourner Truth
sojourner-truth.description:
The Narrative of Sojourner Truth (1850), dictated by Sojourner Truth and edited by Olive Gilbert — the autobiography of one of America's most powerful voices for abolition and women's rights. Born into slavery as Isabella in Ulster County, New York, around 1797, she endured decades of bondage, escaped to freedom, won a landmark legal case to recover her illegally sold son, experienced a profound spiritual transformation, and renamed herself Sojourner Truth as she set out to preach against slavery and injustice. This narrative captures her life from birth through her emergence as a prophetic public figure.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1674 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1674)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs and cartes de visite of Sojourner Truth, particularly the iconic seated portraits (c. 1864) she sold with the caption 'I Sell the Shadow to Support the Substance.' Frontispiece portrait from the 1850 first edition published in Boston. Illustrations from abolitionist publications and anti-slavery almanacs. Engravings from the 1875 expanded edition 'Narrative of Sojourner Truth; a Bondswoman of Olden Time' with added 'Book of Life.'

song-of-songs.name: Song of Songs
song-of-songs.description:
The Song of Songs (Song of Solomon) -- the Bible's great poem of erotic love, in 8 chapters of dialogue between two unnamed lovers. One of the oldest and most beautiful love poems in world literature, it celebrates desire, beauty, longing, and union through the imagery of gardens, vineyards, and the natural world. From the King James Version.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #10900 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/10900)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Marc Chagall's Song of Songs illustrations (1957-1966, check copyright); Gustave Moreau's biblical paintings of love and beauty (1880s); medieval manuscript illuminations of the Song of Songs from the Rothschild Canticles (c. 1300).

songs-of-kabir.name: Songs of Kabir
songs-of-kabir.description:
100 devotional songs by the 15th-century Indian mystic poet Kabir, translated by Rabindranath Tagore with an introduction by Evelyn Underhill. Kabir was born Muslim, studied under a Hindu guru, and refused all religious boundaries: 'I am at once the child of Allah and of Ram.' His songs fuse Sufi ecstasy with Vedantic non-duality — the divine lover is found not in temples or mosques but in the breath, the heartbeat, the ordinary.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #6519 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/6519)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Indian miniature paintings from the Mughal and Rajput traditions. Illustrations from early 20th century editions of Tagore's translations. Simple ink drawings in the Bengali tradition.

sonnets-from-portuguese.name: Sonnets from the Portuguese
sonnets-from-portuguese.description:
44 love sonnets by Elizabeth Barrett Browning, written during her courtship with Robert Browning (1845-1846) and published in 1850. 'How do I love thee? Let me count the ways' (Sonnet XLIII) is among the most famous love poems in English. The title was a disguise — Browning called his wife 'my little Portuguese,' and the poems were presented as translations rather than the deeply personal love letters they truly were.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2002 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2002)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Pre-Raphaelite paintings by Dante Gabriel Rossetti, Edward Burne-Jones. The Rossetti portrait of Elizabeth Siddal. William Morris's floral borders and Arts and Crafts typography.

souls-of-black-folk.name: The Souls of Black Folk
souls-of-black-folk.description:
W. E. B. Du Bois's 'The Souls of Black Folk' (1903) — the book that defined the African American intellectual tradition. In fourteen essays that blend sociology, history, memoir, fiction, and music criticism, Du Bois introduced the concepts of 'the Veil' and 'double consciousness,' challenged Booker T. Washington's program of accommodation, documented the economic realities of the Black Belt, and elevated the Sorrow Songs to their rightful place as America's greatest musical achievement. 'The problem of the Twentieth Century is the problem of the color line.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #408 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/408)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of W. E. B. Du Bois. Illustrations from the 1903 first edition published by A. C. McClurg & Co., Chicago. Photographs of Black Belt Georgia by Du Bois and others from the 1900 Paris Exposition display. Sheet music and notation of the Sorrow Songs.

spirits-book-kardec.name: The Spirits' Book
spirits-book-kardec.description:
The foundational text of Spiritism by Allan Kardec (1857), structured as 1,019 questions posed to spirits and their answers, covering God, the spirit world, reincarnation, moral laws, and the afterlife. Translated by Anna Blackwell (1875). Source: Internet Archive OCR text (https://archive.org/details/AllanKardecTheSpiritsBook). Note: The source text was obtained via OCR from a scanned Internet Archive copy and contains artifacts and garbled passages; chapter summaries have been provided alongside extracted text to ensure readability.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Engravings from 19th-century French Spiritist publications, particularly those from the Revue Spirite (founded by Kardec in 1858). Portraits of Allan Kardec from the 1860s-1870s — widely reproduced, public domain. Illustrations from early editions published by Didier et Cie, Paris. Symbolic and allegorical engravings of spirit communication, table-turning, and the afterlife from 19th-century Spiritualist and Spiritist literature.

story-01-asking-not-complaining.name: Shiryo Learns to Ask — Full Story
story-01-asking-not-complaining.description: The complete page-by-page sequence for Story 1: Shiryo learns that asking is better than complaining. Each page includes narrative text, image status, and generation prompts for AI image creation (Midjourney, DALL-E, Gemini Imagen). Pages without images are marked for generation. The prompts maintain visual consistency with the series style guide defined in the parent grammar.

story-02-gratitude-not-praise.name: Shiryo Learns to Say Thank You — Full Story
story-02-gratitude-not-praise.description: The complete page-by-page sequence for Story 2: Shiryo learns that gratitude is better than praise. Nature wants to be thanked and tended to, not congratulated. Each page includes narrative text and generation prompts for AI image creation (Midjourney, DALL-E, Gemini Imagen).

swedenborg-heaven-hell.name: Heaven and Hell
swedenborg-heaven-hell.description:
Emanuel Swedenborg's visionary account of the afterlife, 'Heaven and its Wonders and Hell' (1758), describing the structure of heaven, the world of spirits, and hell based on his claimed thirteen years of direct spiritual experience. Translated by John C. Ager.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #17368 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/17368)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: William Blake's illustrations inspired by Swedenborg's visions (1790s), particularly from 'The Marriage of Heaven and Hell.' John Flaxman's neoclassical spiritual illustrations. Hieronymus Bosch's 'The Garden of Earthly Delights' (1490-1510) for hellscape imagery. Gustave Doré's illustrations for Dante's Divine Comedy provide parallel afterlife imagery.

symposium-plato.name: Symposium
symposium-plato.description:
Plato's Symposium (c. 385-370 BCE) -- the most perfect dialogue on the nature of love ever written. At a banquet celebrating the tragic poet Agathon's first victory, seven speakers offer speeches in praise of Love (Eros). From Phaedrus' mythological praise through Aristophanes' famous myth of the split humans to Socrates' account of Diotima's 'Ladder of Beauty' -- the ascent from earthly love to the contemplation of Beauty itself -- the Symposium traces every dimension of love: ethical, cosmic, comic, poetic, philosophical, and confessional. Benjamin Jowett translation. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1600 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1600).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Anselm Feuerbach's 'The Symposium' (1869, second version 1873) -- monumental oil painting of the banquet scene, Nationalgalerie Berlin, public domain. Greek red-figure pottery depicting symposia (banquet scenes), widely available from museum collections. Jacques-Louis David's 'The Death of Socrates' (1787) for the figure of Socrates. Raphael's 'The School of Athens' (1509-1511) for Plato's figure. John Flaxman's line illustrations of Greek subjects (early 19th century), public domain.

tao-te-ching.name: Tao Te Ching
tao-te-ching.description:
The Tao Te Ching ('The Classic of the Way and Its Virtue'), attributed to Lao Tzu, is the foundational text of philosophical Taoism. 81 brief chapters on the nameless source of reality, non-action (wu wei), and the paradox of power through yielding. James Legge translation (1891).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #216 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/216)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese ink wash paintings (sumi-e) of mountains, water, and mist. The Ma Yuan and Xia Gui school of Southern Song landscape painting (12th-13th century) captures the Tao Te Ching's aesthetic perfectly — vast empty space with minimal brushstrokes suggesting mountains emerging from void.

tarot-of-all-tarots.name: The Tarot of All Tarots — Five Centuries of the Cards
tarot-of-all-tarots.description:
A meta-tarot holding five public domain decks in dialogue across 78 cards. Each card is a prism: the same archetype seen through the Rider-Waite-Smith (1909), Tarot de Marseille (1760), Sola Busca (1491), three editions of Etteilla (1788/1838/1865), and the Bolognese Tarocchino. Every card includes contextual readings for professional, relationship, and consciousness questions, plus a shadow reading.

The deck images render inline — you see Colman Smith's golden Fool beside Conver's woodcut Le Mat beside the Sola Busca's wild-haired Mato, each carrying the same archetype through different centuries and sensibilities. The three Etteilla editions show how a single deck evolved across 77 years: from Basan's austere engravings (1788) through Blocquel's recycled letterpress (1838) to Regamey's vibrant chromolithographs (1865).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Pamela Colman Smith's original 1909 RWS illustrations (Wikimedia Commons), Nicolas Conver's 1760 Marseille woodcuts (Gallica/BnF via Wikimedia), Sola Busca engravings c. 1491 (Pinacoteca di Brera via Wikimedia), Etteilla I Basan engravings 1788 (Benebell Wen reconstruction), Etteilla II Blocquel letterpress 1838, Etteilla III Regamey chromolithographs 1865, Tarocchino Arlecchino by Yve Lepkowski 2022.

tarot-rws-etteilla.name: The Tarot — Rider-Waite-Smith with Etteilla
tarot-rws-etteilla.description:
A complete 78-card tarot grammar holding two traditions in dialogue. The Rider-Waite-Smith deck (1909) provides the primary structure — 22 Major Arcana and 56 Minor Arcana with upright and reversed interpretations. Inlined alongside each card is its Etteilla correspondence (1788) — the first dedicated cartomancy system, which orders the Major Arcana by the seven days of Genesis rather than the Fool's Journey, and adds Etymology, Historical Context, Hermetic Correspondence, and Genesis Connection sections.

Where RWS asks 'what archetype is active in your life?', Etteilla asks 'what is the cosmos doing?' Both are valid maps of the same territory. This grammar lets you read either or both.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Pamela Colman Smith's original 1909 RWS illustrations (public domain as of 2021 in most jurisdictions), Etteilla's Grand Jeu deck illustrations (1788-1791, public domain), Marseille Tarot woodcuts (17th-18th century, public domain).

the-negro.name: The Negro
the-negro.description:
W. E. B. Du Bois's 'The Negro' (1915) — one of the first comprehensive histories of African peoples written by a Black scholar. In twelve chapters, Du Bois traces the history of Black civilizations from ancient Egypt and Ethiopia through the great kingdoms of West and Central Africa, through the catastrophe of the Atlantic slave trade, to the modern diaspora in the Americas. Written at a time when mainstream scholarship denied Africa any civilizational achievement, this book was a revolutionary act of intellectual recovery.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #15359 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/15359)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Maps from the 1915 first edition published by Henry Holt and Company. Photographs from Du Bois's research. Illustrations of African art, architecture, and artifacts from early 20th century ethnographic collections.

the-prophet.name: The Prophet
the-prophet.description:
The Prophet by Kahlil Gibran (1923) is a collection of 26 poetic essays spoken by the sage Almustafa to the people of Orphalese on the day of his departure. Topics range from Love and Marriage to Death and Freedom. One of the most translated books in history, it offers a non-sectarian mystical vision of human life in all its dimensions.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #58585 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/58585)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Gibran's own twelve original drawings accompany the text — ethereal charcoal and wash figures in his distinctive symbolist style, influenced by William Blake and Auguste Rodin. These drawings are integral to the work and are in the public domain.

theater-tree.name: Theater Tree: The Empty Space
theater-tree.description:
A grammar of theater as embodied practice — not plays, but the art of making theater. From Stanislavski's emotional truth to Grotowski's holy actor, from Brecht's political interruption to Boal's liberation. Each practitioner is a node in a living tree whose roots reach back to Greek ritual and whose branches keep growing. Named for Peter Brook's insight: 'I can take any empty space and call it a bare stage.'

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Ancient Greek theater masks and vase paintings (5th century BCE). Commedia dell'arte engravings by Jacques Callot (1622). Victorian theatrical photographs and playbills. Constructivist stage designs by Meyerhold/Popova (1920s).

through-the-looking-glass.name: Through the Looking-Glass
through-the-looking-glass.description:
Lewis Carroll's beloved sequel to Alice's Adventures in Wonderland (1871), in which Alice steps through a mirror into a world where everything runs backwards. Structured as a chess game, the story follows Alice from Pawn to Queen through encounters with Tweedledum and Tweedledee, Humpty Dumpty, the White Knight, and the Red Queen. Contains some of the most famous poems in the English language, including Jabberwocky and The Walrus and the Carpenter. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #12 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/12).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John Tenniel's original 1871 illustrations for 'Through the Looking-Glass' (Macmillan) -- the definitive visual companion, featuring detailed wood engravings of Alice, Humpty Dumpty, the Jabberwock, the White Knight, and all major characters. Peter Newell's 1902 illustrations (Harper & Brothers) -- softer, more whimsical pen-and-ink drawings. Arthur Rackham's 1907 illustrations for 'Alice's Adventures in Wonderland' (Heinemann) -- while primarily for the first book, Rackham's style perfectly complements the Looking-Glass world. Blanche McManus's 1899 illustrations -- color plates with Art Nouveau influences.

thus-spoke-zarathustra.name: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
thus-spoke-zarathustra.description:
Friedrich Nietzsche's philosophical masterpiece, written between 1883 and 1885 — a prose-poem in four parts in which the prophet Zarathustra descends from mountain solitude to teach humanity about the Overman, the eternal recurrence, and the will to power. Through eighty speeches of extraordinary lyrical force, Nietzsche dismantles conventional morality and calls for a radical affirmation of life. Thomas Common translation (1909). Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1998 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1998).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: No widely known public domain illustration editions exist specifically for Zarathustra, but appropriate visual companions include: Caspar David Friedrich's paintings (1774-1840) — 'Wanderer above the Sea of Fog' and mountain landscapes that capture Zarathustra's solitary heights. William Blake's visionary prints (1757-1827) — prophetic figures and cosmic imagery fitting Nietzsche's tone. Gustave Doré's biblical and literary illustrations (1832-1883) — mountain scenes, prophetic figures, and cosmic vistas.

tibetan-dream-kids.name: The Lands of Sleep: A Dream Guide for Bedtime
tibetan-dream-kids.description:
Tibetan dream yoga and bardo traditions in rhyming verse for bedtime. The ONLY grammar designed to be read AS a bedtime ritual — not just stories about sleep, but stories that accompany the actual process of falling asleep, dreaming, and waking up.

The Bardo Thodol (Tibetan Book of the Dead) describes six intermediate states between death and rebirth. This grammar reframes those bardos as six stages of a night's sleep: the threshold of falling asleep, the peaceful lands of gentle dreams, the wrathful guardians of nightmares (reframed as protectors wearing masks), the mirror of lucid dreaming, the deep of dreamless sleep, and the dawn of waking.

Reading instructions: At bedtime, read from The Threshold. For nightmare preparation, read from The Wrathful Guardians. For lucid dreaming curiosity, read from The Mirror. In the morning, read from The Dawn.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Tibetan thangka paintings from the Met and Rubin Museum (open access) — luminous peaceful and wrathful deities. Odilon Redon (1840-1916) — French Symbolist pastels, the most dream-like art in Western tradition. Paul Klee (1879-1940) — childlike geometry and color-as-mood landscapes. Hilma af Klint (1862-1944) — Swedish mystic painter, abstract spiritual visions and mandala-like compositions.

tibetan-dream-kids-storyboard.name: The Lands of Sleep: A Dream Guide for Bedtime — Storyboard
tibetan-dream-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on The Lands of Sleep: A Dream Guide for Bedtime. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

tibetan-tales.name: Tibetan Tales, Derived from Indian Sources
tibetan-tales.description:
Fifty tales from the Tibetan Kah-gyur, translated by F. Anton von Schiefner and rendered into English by W. R. S. Ralston (1906). Jataka tales, animal fables, stories of kings and saints — the Buddhist narrative tradition as it travelled from India to Tibet.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #66870 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/66870)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: No illustrations in the original edition. Tibetan thangka painting traditions and Indian Jataka relief carvings from Sanchi, Bharhut, and Ajanta would provide appropriate visual companions.

timaeus-plato.name: Timaeus
timaeus-plato.description:
Plato's Timaeus in Benjamin Jowett's translation — the most influential cosmological text of antiquity. A divine Craftsman creates the universe as a living, intelligent being with a mathematically structured World Soul. Time is a moving image of eternity. Matter is composed of geometric triangles. The human being is a microcosm of the cosmos. Contains the earliest account of Atlantis. Includes Jowett's Introduction and Analysis.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #1572 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1572). Benjamin Jowett translation.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Raphael's 'School of Athens' (1509-1511, Vatican), showing Plato holding the Timaeus. Medieval diagrams of the Platonic solids and World Soul. Robert Fludd's Utriusque Cosmi (1617) cosmological engravings.

tom-sawyer.name: The Adventures of Tom Sawyer
tom-sawyer.description:
Mark Twain's The Adventures of Tom Sawyer (1876) -- the great American novel of boyhood. Tom Sawyer whitewashes a fence, falls in love, witnesses a murder, runs away to be a pirate, attends his own funeral, testifies at a murder trial, gets lost in a cave, and finds buried treasure -- all in one summer along the Mississippi River. Thirty-five chapters of comedy, adventure, and surprising moral depth.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #74 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/74).

Note on racial content: The character of Injun Joe reflects 19th-century racial stereotypes of Native Americans. Twain uses 'Injun' and presents the character through the lens of period prejudices. These elements require critical discussion, particularly with young readers.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- True Williams (1876, American Publishing Company) -- original illustrator, over 160 pen-and-ink drawings capturing every scene of small-town Missouri life
- E.W. Kemble (1884, Chatto & Windus) -- British edition illustrator
- Worth Brehm (1910, Harper & Brothers) -- classic early 20th-century color plates
- Norman Rockwell (1936, Heritage Press) -- iconic American realist illustrations

up-from-slavery.name: Up from Slavery
up-from-slavery.description:
Booker T. Washington's 'Up from Slavery: An Autobiography' (1901) — the most widely read African American autobiography of its era. Born into slavery in Virginia, Washington worked his way through Hampton Institute, founded Tuskegee Institute in Alabama, and became the most influential Black leader in America after his 1895 Atlanta Exposition Address. This autobiography is both an inspiring narrative of self-made achievement and a political document arguing for industrial education and racial accommodation — a philosophy that W. E. B. Du Bois would challenge in 'The Souls of Black Folk' two years later.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2376 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2376)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of Booker T. Washington. Images of Tuskegee Institute from its early decades. Photographs from the Atlanta Cotton States Exposition of 1895. Portraits by Frances Benjamin Johnston and other period photographers.

upanishads.name: The Upanishads
upanishads.description:
Three principal Upanishads — Isha, Katha, and Kena — translated from Sanskrit by Swami Paramananda (1919). The Upanishads ('sitting near the teacher') are the philosophical conclusion of the Vedas, exploring the nature of Brahman (ultimate reality), Atman (the Self), and their identity. These texts are the fountainhead of Vedanta philosophy and the deepest roots of yoga, meditation, and non-dual awareness. Each verse is presented with Paramananda's commentary.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #3283 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/3283)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Rajput miniature paintings of sages and forest ashrams from the 17th-18th centuries. Illustrations from early 20th century Vedanta Society publications. Traditional Indian manuscript illuminations depicting scenes of guru-disciple teaching in forest settings.

varieties-religious-experience.name: The Varieties of Religious Experience
varieties-religious-experience.description:
William James's The Varieties of Religious Experience: A Study in Human Nature (1902) — the founding text of the psychology of religion. Twenty Gifford Lectures delivered at Edinburgh, taking a radical empirical approach to religious experience: conversion, mysticism, saintliness, the sick soul, healthy-mindedness. James collects vivid first-person accounts of religious states and subjects them to psychological analysis and pragmatic evaluation, concluding that they point to a 'wider self' beyond ordinary consciousness.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #621 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/621)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Photographs of William James (various, 1890s-1900s). Images of the University of Edinburgh and McEwan Hall where the Gifford Lectures were delivered. Portraits of figures discussed: George Fox, John Bunyan, Teresa of Avila, Tolstoy.

vindication-rights-woman.name: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
vindication-rights-woman.description:
Mary Wollstonecraft's revolutionary treatise on women's education and rights (1792) -- the founding text of feminist philosophy. In thirteen chapters, Wollstonecraft argues that women are not naturally inferior but are made so by an education designed to produce ornamental dependence. She calls for equal education, co-educational public schooling, and the recognition of women as rational beings capable of virtue and citizenship.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #3420 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/3420)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: John Opie's portrait of Mary Wollstonecraft (c. 1797, National Portrait Gallery); William Blake's engravings for Wollstonecraft's 'Original Stories from Real Life' (1791); James Gillray's political caricatures of 1790s radical intellectuals.

voices-of-the-unwritten.name: Voices of the Unwritten: The Sages Who Never Wrote & The Disciples Who Carried the Fire
voices-of-the-unwritten.description:
A tarot deck of the sages who never wrote a word. Every tradition that shaped civilization — Buddhism, Christianity, Confucianism, Stoicism, Sufism, the Tao — began in someone's mouth, not on someone's page. These sages trusted voice over text, presence over permanence, silence over scripture. Their disciples became the first scribes, carrying fire in clay jars, inevitably distorting what they preserved. The 22 Sages form the Major Arcana, mapped to the Hero's Journey and Hajo Banzhaf's 'The Path.' The Disciples form their own suit — the human hands that caught the divine overflow. Together they ask: what is lost when the living word becomes the written word? What is gained? And who decides which version of the silence gets printed?

Spread: The Path (after Hajo Banzhaf). Three rows of seven cards plus The Fool wandering between them. Row 1 (The Outer Journey): Magician through Chariot. Row 2 (The Inner Journey): Strength through Temperance. Row 3 (The Return): Devil through World. The Fool walks alongside all three, belonging to none.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Ancient Greek vase paintings of philosophers and oracles. Buddhist Gandhara sculpture (2nd-3rd century CE). Medieval manuscript illuminations of Desert Fathers. Mughal miniatures of Sufi saints. Hokusai and Hiroshige prints of sages. William Blake's visionary engravings. Gustave Doré's biblical illustrations.

voyage-of-the-beagle.name: The Voyage of the Beagle
voyage-of-the-beagle.description:
Charles Darwin's journal of his five-year circumnavigation aboard HMS Beagle (1831-1836) — one of the greatest travel and natural history books ever written. In 21 chapters following the voyage from the Cape Verde Islands through South America, the Galapagos, the Pacific, and home, Darwin records the geology, wildlife, and peoples he encountered. These observations laid the groundwork for the theory of evolution by natural selection.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #944 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/944)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Conrad Martens' watercolors and sketches from the Beagle voyage. John Gould's ornithological illustrations from The Zoology of the Voyage of H.M.S. Beagle (1838-1843). Robert FitzRoy's charts and coastal profiles. Period maps of the Beagle's route.

walden.name: Walden
walden.description:
Henry David Thoreau's Walden (1854) — the foundational text of American nature writing and voluntary simplicity. A record of two years spent living in a self-built cabin on the shore of Walden Pond near Concord, Massachusetts. Thoreau distills his experiment into 18 chapters that move from the economics of shelter and food through deep nature observation to spiritual awakening. 'I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately, to front only the essential facts of life.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #205 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/205)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Illustrations from the first edition of Walden (Ticknor and Fields, 1854) including Thoreau's own survey map of Walden Pond. Daguerreotypes of Thoreau (1856). Photographs of Walden Pond and Concord landscapes. Herbert Wendell Gleason's photographs of Thoreau country (early 1900s).

west-african-folk-tales.name: West African Folk-Tales
west-african-folk-tales.description:
Thirty-six folk tales from the Gold Coast (modern Ghana), collected and arranged by W. H. Barker and Cecilia Sinclair (1917). Divided into Anansi (Spider) tales and miscellaneous tales, this collection preserves the oral traditions of the Akan, Fanti, and Ashanti peoples. Anansi the Spider — the great trickster of West African folklore — dominates the first half, while the second half features tales of talking animals, magical transformations, and moral lessons. These are the stories that traveled with enslaved Africans to become the Brer Rabbit tales of the American South and the Anansi stories of the Caribbean. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #66923 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/66923).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Cecilia Sinclair's twenty-three original pen-and-ink drawings for the 1917 George G. Harrap & Company edition — charming line illustrations depicting native children, Anansi's adventures, and animal characters. The frontispiece shows 'Native Children ready for a Story.'

west-african-kids.name: Spider's Thread: Stories from West Africa
west-african-kids.description:
Forty-two bedtime stories in rhyming verse, drawn from West African mythology — Akan/Ashanti trickster tales, Yoruba Orisha traditions, and Igbo wisdom — retold for children with esoteric annotations for curious grown-ups. Anansi the Spider bought all the world's stories from the Sky God, and these are some of the threads he spun.

Cultural note: Orisha worship is a living tradition practiced by millions across West Africa, Brazil, Cuba, Haiti, Trinidad, and the United States. These stories honor that tradition with respect, not as museum artifacts but as living fire. The Whisper notes reference scholarly sources for parents who want to go deeper.

Sources: Barker & Sinclair, 'West African Folk-Tales' (1917); R.S. Rattray, 'Akan-Ashanti Folk-Tales' (1930); Pierre Fatumbi Verger, Yoruba Orisha traditions.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Adinkra symbols from traditional Ashanti textile printing (geometric black-on-red stamp patterns, each encoding a proverb or concept). Benin Bronzes — Met Museum Open Access collection (portrait heads, warrior plaques, leopard figures, 16th-18th century cast brass). Early 20th century illustrations from Barker & Sinclair's 'West African Folk-Tales' (1917, line drawings of Anansi, animals, village scenes). Kente cloth patterns (Ashanti/Ewe woven textile geometry, public domain photographs). Ashanti gold weights (small brass figurines depicting proverbs and daily life, Met Museum Open Access).

west-african-kids-storyboard.name: Spider's Thread: Stories from West Africa — Storyboard
west-african-kids-storyboard.description: Production storyboard for animated series based on Spider's Thread: Stories from West Africa. Each story is split into 4-8 scenes with narration arcs, AI image generation prompts, camera directions, and public domain illustration references. Designed for 5-minute AI-animated episodes in the style of Mati and Dada.

wildflowers-of-california.name: Wildflowers of California
wildflowers-of-california.description:
A field guide to 30 beloved California wildflowers — from the golden poppies of spring hillsides to the desert lilies of the Mojave. Each flower is a doorway into California's extraordinary ecological diversity, with tiered content for young children (Story) and older readers (Science). Organized by habitat: coast, valley, foothill, mountain, and desert.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Mary Elizabeth Parsons' 'The Wild Flowers of California' (1897, Cunningham, Curtiss & Welch) with illustrations by Margaret Warriner Buck — delicate, accurate botanical line drawings. Albert Kellogg (1813–1887), founding member of the California Academy of Sciences, botanical illustrations of California flora.

winnie-the-pooh-chapter-book.name: Winnie-the-Pooh — A Chapter Book
winnie-the-pooh-chapter-book.description: A. A. Milne's beloved classic broken into scenes for multi-level reading. Level 1 scenes contain the complete original text. Level 2 chapters provide brief summaries. Based on the 1926 first edition, now in the public domain.

wonderful-wizard-of-oz.name: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
wonderful-wizard-of-oz.description:
L. Frank Baum's The Wonderful Wizard of Oz (1900) -- the first great American fairy tale. Dorothy, a Kansas farm girl, is carried by a cyclone to the Land of Oz, where she meets the Scarecrow, the Tin Woodman, and the Cowardly Lion. Together they follow the yellow brick road to the Emerald City, seeking brains, a heart, courage, and a way home -- only to discover that a humbug wizard and a pair of Silver Shoes hold truths about what we already have within us. Twenty-four chapters structured as story arcs and thematic groupings.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #55 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/55). Text matches the original 1900 George M. Hill Company edition.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES:
- W.W. Denslow (1900, George M. Hill Company) -- original illustrator, iconic color plates of Dorothy, the Scarecrow, the Tin Woodman, and the Cowardly Lion in bold Art Nouveau style
- John R. Neill (1904 onward, Reilly & Britton) -- illustrated all subsequent Oz books, elegant pen-and-ink drawings with more detailed, whimsical style

yoga-body-as-temple.name: Yoga: Body as Temple
yoga-body-as-temple.description:
A grammar of yoga as embodied philosophy — from individual asanas to the eight limbs of Patanjali. Each pose carries its Sanskrit story, its physical opening, and its place in a 5,000-year tradition of using the body as an instrument of awareness. Covers ~50 asanas across 7 families, the 8 limbs of classical yoga, and 3 meta-perspectives on what yoga is and where it's going.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Illustrations from early 20th-century translations of the Hatha Yoga Pradipika. Woodcuts from the Yoga Korunta tradition. Indian miniature paintings depicting yogic postures (Mughal and Rajput schools, 17th-18th century).
